# Summary

A 64-year-old former school principal from Japan has been arrested and faces criminal charges after police say he paid for sex with over 12,000 women, including girls as young as 14, during repeated visits to the Philippines over more than 25 years. According to Japanese police, Yuhei Takashima was arrested on Wednesday for allegedly photographing an obscene act with a 13 or 14-year-old girl and producing pornography in Manila, with officers seizing over 147,600 photos documenting his activities. Police say Takashima began paying for sex in 1988 while working at a Japanese school in Manila and continued the practice for decades afterward, citing the low cost and relief from work pressure.

# Summary

A Brazilian online football store is selling what it claims to be next season's Chelsea kits featuring the incoming 'Yokohama' sponsor on the front, though without the official Adidas emblem. Most notably, the alleged new kit can be purchased with Gareth Bale's name and number 9 on the back, fueling speculation among Chelsea supporters about a potential transfer for the Welsh international from Real Madrid. The website has published three Chelsea kit designs, though their authenticity remains questionable given the absence of the club's official kit manufacturer branding.

# Summary

Clarence Taylor, 44, remains at large after police in Akron, Ohio charged him with faking his own kidnapping in a bizarre incident last fall. On November 1, Taylor's girlfriend reported him missing, but he was soon found tied to a tree with zip ties, claiming he had been abducted and robbed of $2,500 by armed men. Despite showing no signs of injury or dehydration consistent with being exposed to the elements for several days, police charged Taylor with a first-degree misdemeanor for falsification, and a warrant for his arrest was issued last month.

# Summary

Rita Wilson and an ensemble of actresses created a satirical video for the Make it Fair Project that uses irony to highlight gender inequality, sarcastically singing "It's only fair that men should have it all" while presenting statistics showing men hold 70-95% of important positions in industries like Hollywood, where they directed 93% of films and wrote 80% in recent years. The video emphasizes that gender disparity extends far beyond entertainment, noting that men comprise 95% of Fortune 500 CEOs, 88% of Congress, and earn 30% more than women despite women making up 60% of college graduates. To underscore the wealth of available female talent, the entire PSA was written, produced, and starred by women.

# Summary

A warming climate approximately 1.4 million years ago enabled our distant human ancestors, known as hominins, to venture from Africa into Western Europe for the first time. Researchers believe that colder and drier temperatures had previously halted the spread of these early hominins, but when conditions warmed to 13°C and became more humid, they were able to inhabit southeast Spain and ultimately spread across Europe. The study suggests that climate change directly impacted human migration by affecting the availability of resources in new regions.

# Summary

Former NFL running back Lawrence Phillips, who has been incarcerated since 2005 serving more than 31 years for choking his girlfriend in San Diego and driving his car into three teens after a pickup football game in Los Angeles, is suspected of killing his cellmate Damion Soward at Kern Valley State Prison. Soward, a 37-year-old inmate serving 82 years to life for first-degree murder, was found dead in the cell he shared with Phillips on Saturday morning, with the suspected killing revealed by prison officials on Monday. Prison officials are investigating the death along with the Kern County Coroner and Kern County District Attorney, while also investigating a separate suspected cellmate killing at the same facility.

# Summary

Liverpool's FA Cup semi-final defeat to Aston Villa has exposed significant problems at the club, including manager Brendan Rodgers' tactical inconsistency in crucial matches and a transfer committee that has squandered money on poor signings, with only three of 17 players delivering results over two summers. With key players departing, injuries plaguing striker Daniel Sturridge, and the squad lacking the mental resilience and character needed to win close matches, Liverpool faces a critical summer where they must rebuild both their squad and mentality to avoid continued mediocrity.

# Summary

Google celebrated April Fools' Day a day early by combining Google Maps with Pac-Man, allowing users to play the classic game on real-world locations like the Champs Elysees, Broadway, and the Embarcadero. The tech company, known for its impish April Fools' pranks, made the game easily accessible by adding a Pac-Man icon to Google Maps on desktop browsers. Twitter users have been excited about the possibilities, playing Pac-Man in various locations around the world including Manhattan, London, and San Francisco.

# Summary

Rangers boss Stuart McCall has reassured fans that the club's removal from the London Stock Exchange's AIM market will not impact their promotion push, despite the Ibrox faithful being rocked by the delisting after chairman-in-waiting Dave King failed to secure a new nominated advisor. Interim chairman Paul Murray blamed the club's previous board for AIM's decision while revealing the new regime is considering a switch to the ISDX market. McCall also expressed surprise at learning Rangers must pay Newcastle United £500,000 if promoted, but remains focused on the immediate challenge of securing promotion and giving Hearts a guard of honour after their remarkable 26 wins from 30 games.

# Summary

The US body that regulates Internet addresses, ICANN, is cracking down on a Canadian company, Vox Populi Registry Ltd., that is using the new '.sucks' domain name to extract money from celebrities and companies seeking to protect their reputations. Since the '.sucks' domain opened on March 30, major brands like Visa, YouTube, and Bank of America have had their names registered, and celebrities including Kevin Spacey have reportedly paid to have their names removed from these domains. ICANN has referred the company's actions to US and Canadian regulators to investigate whether the practice constitutes an illegal "shakedown scheme."

# Summary

Edinson Cavani's poor performance in PSG's Champions League defeat to Barcelona has likely ended any prospect of him joining Manchester United to fill their striker vacancy at Old Trafford, as the chances were always slim to begin with. Manager Louis van Gaal, who prefers working with younger players he can develop, has been wary of signing aging South American strikers following Radamel Falcao's unsuccessful loan spell, and Cavani's inconsistent goal-scoring record in Ligue 1 has done little to change his mind. Despite the speculation, Cavani has insisted he intends to respect his PSG contract and remain in France to help the club's title fight.

# Premier League Relegation Battle Summary

The fight to avoid relegation remains wide open, with no team dead and buried despite Sunderland sitting in 15th place just seven points above the drop zone. The battle is extremely close among the bottom six sides, with only five key fixtures between them potentially deciding who survives, making it unclear whether teams like Hull can overcome their hardest run-in to safety. While predictions suggest Sunderland and Aston Villa should survive, Leicester and Burnley face the most difficult paths to survival, with both forecasted to be relegated based on their challenging remaining fixtures and poor current form.

# Summary

Karim Benzema demonstrated his talents extend beyond the football pitch when the Real Madrid star posted a video of himself rapping along to Tupac's "Check Out Time" while driving his Bugatti Veyron, though Tupac's "All Eyez on Me" might have been a more fitting choice as Benzema looks to add to his 20-goal tally this season. The Los Blancos forward also expressed confidence that he can win the Ballon d'Or award in the future if he continues to perform at a high level for Real Madrid and France, despite acknowledging that Cristiano Ronaldo and Lionel Messi currently dominate the award.

# Summary

Pope Francis has been told by Vatican doctors to cut down on pasta to just two servings a week and take daily walks to address his weight gain and sedentary lifestyle. The 78-year-old Catholic leader's doctors are concerned that his considerable weight gain since taking office is aggravating his sciatica, a nerve condition causing back pain, particularly given his demanding travel schedule. Despite the medical advice, the Pope has joked that he is "the undisciplined one" and continues to eat pasta daily, largely ignoring his doctors' warnings.

# Summary

Google has released a new voice unlock feature for Android that allows users to unlock their phones by saying "OK, Google," though the company warns this method is less secure than traditional passwords and someone with a similar voice could gain access to the device. Additionally, Google offers Face Unlock technology, which uses facial recognition to unlock smartphones and tablets, and was updated with a "liveness" check that requires users to blink to prove they are alive and prevent unauthorized access through static photographs. Google also provides two-step verification for user accounts to enhance security across its Android devices.

# Summary

Robert Bates' lawyer, Scott Wood, released documents on Saturday claiming to verify that the 73-year-old Oklahoma reserve deputy met all required training to be a law enforcement officer, including one Taser training class and multiple firearms qualifications between July 22, 2008, and December 12, 2014. However, the documents' authenticity could not be independently confirmed by CNN, and questions remain about Bates' training records, particularly regarding allegations that some supervisors in the Tulsa County Sheriff's Office were asked to forge his records and were reassigned when they refused. Bates is charged with second-degree manslaughter in the April 2 death of Eric Harris, which he claims was an accidental shooting when he meant to use his Taser.

# Summary

A leading headmaster has urged parents to keep their distance and take a back seat in their children's education, arguing that excessive parental interference has become counterproductive and hindering natural development. Peter Tait, headmaster of Sherborne Preparatory School, wrote that parents should trust schools and teachers instead of acting as "dervishes ready to battle with anyone and anything on behalf of their child," claiming modern parenting trends stem from parents being bombarded with information about how to raise children. The article also highlights concerning data showing that teacher abuse from parents has more than doubled, with 30 percent of teachers experiencing abusive social media posts, underscoring the need for parents to allow professionals to do their job without interference.

# Summary

Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu criticized the Iran nuclear deal framework announced by six world powers, telling CNN's State of the Union that he sees a "third alternative" of standing firm and increasing pressure rather than accepting "this bad deal or war." President Barack Obama endorsed the deal, which would lift Western sanctions in exchange for Iran reducing its centrifuges and allowing increased inspections, but GOP contenders for the 2016 presidential nomination argued it gave Iran too much flexibility. Democrats and Republicans continued sparring on Sunday, with Democratic Senator Dianne Feinstein urging Netanyahu to "contain himself," while Republican Senator Lindsey Graham suggested a new president could negotiate a better deal.

# Summary

Catalans Dragons have suffered a significant blow with winger Vincent Duport, a France international who scored three tries in the first six rounds, ruled out for the rest of the season following a ruptured shoulder tendon sustained in their 33-22 defeat to Hull FC on March 20. The injury requires surgery, and head coach Laurent Frayssinous described the news as "hugely disappointing" for both the player and the team, though he expressed confidence in Duport's ability to recover.

# Summary

Police discovered a body they believe to be Samantha Fleming, an Indiana woman reported missing with her newborn daughter Serenity weeks ago in Gary, a city 180 miles away from their home in Anderson. Fleming and her three-week-old daughter left on April 5 after a woman claiming to be from Indiana's Department of Child Services told Fleming she needed to appear at a court hearing. While the infant was found unharmed and will be returned to relatives, the body discovered at the home was found doused with bleach, wrapped in plastic, and stuffed in a storage bin, according to police.

# Summary

A geologist in Indonesia claims that Gunung Padang, a megalithic site discovered in West Java in 1914, is actually an ancient pyramid hidden beneath rubble that could be 9,000 to 20,000 years old—making it the world's oldest if true. If verified, this discovery would rewrite prehistory and prove that an advanced and obscure ancient civilization existed in the region. However, the claim remains controversial, with 34 Indonesian archaeologists and geologists disputing Dr. Danny Hilman's findings, arguing that the structure may be a natural volcano neck rather than man-made.

# Summary

Shocking footage has emerged of the moment a man stabbed a bouncer outside a Melbourne nightclub just before the Easter long weekend, with Police expressing their disgust over the cowardly attack. According to Police, the man had been ejected from the club late Thursday night when he soon returned and attacked the security guard from behind, leaving the 29-year-old with non-life threatening injuries. Police are seeking two suspects, with one described as wearing a maroon t-shirt and the other in a blue/purple shirt, as a separate stabbing incident also occurred at another Melbourne nightclub on Good Friday.

# Summary

Carlisle United manager Keith Curle has launched a scathing attack on his side following their 3-1 defeat to Accrington Stanley, even suggesting that some players "don't deserve to be professionals." The Cumbrians are without a win in five games and sit just two places and three points above the relegation zone in League Two, with Curle criticizing the lack of strength of character in the changing room. The 51-year-old manager is considering staging an open forum where supporters could confront the players about their lack of commitment to the shirt.

# Victoria's Secret Dream Angels Campaign Summary

Victoria's Secret Angels Candice Swanepoel, Elsa Hosk, and Jasmine Tookes star in the brand's latest glamorous campaign, proving that getting caught in the rain is a far more stylish affair when you're a supermodel. The three models don see-through macs and push-up bras while guiding each other through an energetic routine to promote the brand's new Dream Angels underwear range in blush pink and crisp white tones. Each girl carries a pink striped umbrella—a free gift with the purchase of two bras—which will become the season's must-have accessory.

# Summary

Mila Kunis is being sued by aspiring singer Kristina Karo for allegedly stealing her pet chicken named "Doggie" when they were children in Ukraine over 25 years ago. Karo claims that a seven-year-old Kunis was envious of the hen and confessed to taking it, an incident that she says has traumatized her and prevented her from pursuing her dreams. Karo is seeking $5,000 in damages and has demanded the money cover emotional distress and therapy bills, though court records suggest the two grew up more than 100 miles apart in Ukraine.

# Summary

Richard Howarth, a 35-year-old father from Milton Keynes, claims he has developed an allergic reaction to Ukip leader Nigel Farage, sweating and shaking whenever he sees him on TV, forcing him to change the channel. He says the lead up to the election is particularly difficult because Mr Farage appears on television so often, and seeing him makes him physically ill and anxious about how the party might affect his son's future. A psychologist suggests that Mr Howarth's reaction is more likely a phobia than an allergy, as he experiences an irrational fear response to someone whose political views he fundamentally disagrees with.

# Summary

A 17-year-old intoxicated boy named Rahul Kumar made a sudden dash into a lions' enclosure at a zoo in Ahmedabad, India, surprising zoo security as he ran towards the animals shouting he would kill them. Fortunately, Kumar fell into one of eight moats that separate visitors from the lions, allowing guards to catch up with him and take him out before reaching the predators. Kumar later explained he was drunk and thought he'd stand a good chance against the lions, and was subsequently handed over to police for psychiatric evaluation.

# Summary

Easter chocolate eggs contain alarming amounts of sugar and calories, with a Kit Kat Chunky egg containing 590 calories that requires three 45-minute exercise classes to burn off. A nutritionist warns that consuming these treats can lead to obesity, type 2 diabetes, and nutrient deficiencies, as many popular eggs exceed the World Health Organisation's recommended daily sugar intake. She recommends choosing solid chocolate eggs over those with sugary fillings and eating modest portions to enjoy Easter responsibly.

# Summary

The Havasupai tribe, the smallest Indian nation in America with over 600 members, is hidden away in the depths of the Grand Canyon, though few visitors who witness the spectacular landscape know about this mysterious community. Visitors can reach the remote village of Supai, located eight miles from the nearest road, via a challenging hike through rugged terrain, by mule, or by helicopter, where they can experience village life that includes a cafe, school, and other essential services. The tribe relies heavily on tourism income, with about 20,000 visitors annually traveling to discover their sacred homeland and experience the stunning natural beauty of Havasu Falls.

# Summary

The RTS,S vaccine, which took 30 years to develop, is now hoped to save millions of lives from malaria. GlaxoSmithKline has applied for a licence from the European Medicines Agency (EMA), marking a significant milestone as the first malaria vaccine to reach advanced trials. Tests on over 15,500 babies and toddlers in sub-Saharan Africa showed that those receiving three doses plus a booster saw the number of clinical malaria cases reduced by 36 per cent over four years, with researchers saying it could make a substantial contribution to controlling the disease.

# Summary

Chilean pickpockets have become the most effective thieves operating in central London, according to undercover police teams who report that gangs of up to six follow wealthy shoppers in the West End, stealing their cash and belongings. In one notable case, Metropolitan Police stopped two Chilean women in the Park Lane and Mayfair areas who were carrying a bag containing £20,000, €130,000, and gold and diamond jewellery. These organized international criminals are particularly difficult to trace as they operate in mixed teams disguised as families or businessmen and travel between major European cities to avoid detection.

# Summary

After almost 90 years, the UK's thousands of 165ft electricity pylons that have spoiled scenes of beauty are being replaced by a more elegant design called the T-pylon, which National Grid revealed yesterday. The new T-pylon is about a third shorter than the old lattice pylons but can still carry 400,000 volts of electricity, and National Grid is building a training line of six at their Eakring academy in Nottinghamshire. Unlike the original pylons introduced in 1928, the new design is sleeker and can be erected in just one day, making it ideal for supporting clean energy infrastructure like the planned Hinkley Point C nuclear power station.

# Summary

Holly Nicole Solomon, 31, has pleaded guilty to aggravated assault after hitting her husband Daniel with their SUV in a Mesa parking lot following an argument over his failure to vote in the 2012 presidential election. Solomon, who was six months pregnant at the time, claimed her family would face hardship due to Barack Obama's reelection and said she intended to scare her husband but accidentally hit the accelerator instead of the brake, striking him and causing a shattered pelvis and torn artery. As part of a plea deal, she will serve three and a half years in prison instead of facing up to 15 years if convicted.

# Summary

A 21-year-old student named Drew Hollinshead was issued a £70 parking ticket after he pulled into a disabled bay to help an elderly woman he saw fall to her feet on the pavement in Bournemouth. Though the traffic warden witnessed him helping the lady for less than a minute, he still issued the ticket, prompting Hollinshead to appeal on social media, where supporters criticized Parking Services for "slapping out tickets" without common sense and noted the council only cares about revenue. Bournemouth Council later responded by suggesting Hollinshead appeal the ticket and stating they consider mitigating circumstances in individual cases.

# Summary

Workers in Pingjiang County, Hunan province, are constructing a mountain road on the Mufu mountain range thousands of feet above the ground, hauling heavy planks and wheelbarrows of cement across flimsy wooden walkways and rusty scaffolding poles with minimal safety equipment. Images show the builders precariously extending the walkway by hammering scaffolding into the cliff face to create a concrete path. Chinese officials have tried to capitalize on the area's popularity by building these scenic tourist attractions, hoping to draw thousands of visitors to experience the well-known mountain region.

# Summary

An independent investigation has uncovered a catalogue of failings at two NHS hospitals where seven babies and three mothers died over an eight-month period. The review found notable absence of clinical leadership and failure to adequately plan care at Royal Oldham Hospital, where four babies and two mothers died, and North Manchester General Hospital, where three babies and one mother died, with cases including 35-year-old Lisa Parkisson who died 48 hours after giving birth in April. The investigation revealed that national guidelines for treating obese mothers were not followed and recommended improvements including strengthening clinical leadership, better risk management, and adequate staffing levels.

# Summary

Following months of unrest after Michael Brown's death, Ferguson held municipal elections that resulted in significant change, with two African-American candidates winning seats to make the city council 50% black, reflecting the city's 70% black population. Wesley Bell, a lawyer and criminal justice professor, won his Ward 3 race with nearly 67% of the vote and prioritized community policing reform, stating he wants police officers to be judged by the number of people they know in the community rather than tickets issued, a direct response to a Department of Justice report that found systemic discrimination in law enforcement and municipal court under the former chief Tommy Jackson. The election saw higher-than-normal 30% turnout, with voters motivated by a desire to reform Ferguson's practice of using law enforcement as a revenue stream and to address the institutional problems highlighted by Brown's death.

# Summary

Aaron Finch, Australia's World Cup-winning batsman and captain of the Twenty20 team, underwent surgery on his left hamstring in Melbourne on Tuesday after sustaining a high-grade injury while playing for the Mumbai Indians in the IPL. According to Cricket Australia team physiotherapist Alex Kountouris, the pugnacious opener is expected to miss up to three months as he undergoes rehabilitation. The 28-year-old's injury will sideline him from upcoming international commitments, including the West Indies series and the Ashes tour.

# Summary

AP McCoy secured his second win at Aintree on Don Cossack in the Melling Chase, defeating a horse trained by Gordon Elliott—the same trainer McCoy is praying doesn't win Saturday's Grand National with Cause of Causes, a gelding owned by his boss JP McManus. Jockey Brian Hughes, who took a fall while riding as a guest on the Daresbury estate but considered it a lucky omen, won on Cyrius Darius and will ride outsider Ely Brown in the National, while Irish jockey Robbie McNamara was hospitalized following a fall and is now doubtful to ride top weight Lord Windermere, and Wayne Hutchinson must receive medical clearance to ride Godsmejudge after being stood down from his remaining rides yesterday following a fall from Balder Succes in the Melling Chase.

# Summary

Vince Cable, the Business Secretary and least popular member of the Conservative-Liberal Democrat Coalition Cabinet, takes pride in being the Tories' bogeyman despite his familiar hangdog smile and willingness to work with them. Though David Cameron and George Osborne regard him as a closet Labour politician—unsurprisingly, given that he was once a Labour councillor in the 1970s—Cable indicates he is willing to spend another five years helping the Tories govern and even aspires to replace George Osborne as Chancellor. His pragmatic willingness to work across party lines contrasts sharply with his sharp criticism of Labour leader Ed Miliband's response to the financial crisis and university tuition fees policy.

# Summary

A survey of 2,000 British workers found that the average employee will sit through 6,239 meetings over their career, with 60 per cent finding most meetings "pretty pointless." It is therefore unsurprising that one in five adults admits to catching forty winks during work meetings, with 70 per cent saying they constantly zone out after just 20 minutes. The study reveals that workers often feel their time is wasted in meetings, with many suggesting that conference calls would be more efficient and cost-effective alternatives.

# Summary

A logic puzzle from the Singapore and Asian Schools Math Olympiad asked when Cheryl's birthday is by having her give Albert the month and Bernard the day from a list of 10 possible dates, then having the two men make deductive statements that allowed them to narrow down the answer to July 16. Through a series of logical steps—eliminating months with unique numbers (May and June), ruling out duplicate dates (14th), and deducing that Albert's remaining date must be the only one left in his originally told month—participants could filter out the correct birthday. Around 28,000 students from countries including Singapore, China, Vietnam, and the UK took the test, with organisers using this difficult question as a means to sift out the most intelligent students.

# Summary

An Army major and his wife are on trial for abusing their three foster children between 2005 and 2010 at Picatinny Arsenal military installation, according to a 15-count superseding indictment that alleges they subjected the children to a "regimen of abuse and neglect" resulting in broken bones and serious health problems. The abuse was exposed when one of their biological children, now a teenager, reported it to someone outside the family, while their three biological children showed no signs of abuse. This is the couple's second trial after their first ended in a mistrial when a prosecutor inadvertently mentioned the death of one of the foster children.

# Summary

European papers report on major talking points across the continent, with Spanish newspapers highlighting that Real Madrid's French striker Karim Benzema, who has scored 15 La Liga goals this season, is set to miss the Champions League quarter-final clash against rivals Atletico Madrid due to a knee injury he sustained in Madrid's 3-1 victory against Malaga. Spanish outlets also report that fellow players Gareth Bale and Luka Modric are likely to miss Wednesday's clash, forcing manager Carlo Ancelotti to reconsider his tactical formation. Additionally, Barcelona coach Luis Enrique is being hailed for having the best statistical record in his first 50 games, while Italian papers celebrate Juventus's march toward the Serie A title following their victory over Lazio.

# Summary

The culprits behind the Hatton Garden raid may have carried out a similarly audacious burglary in Berlin just two years earlier, according to emerging evidence. In January 2013, thieves targeted 294 security vaults at the Volksbank in Steglitz, Berlin, fleeing with diamonds, gold, and silver worth over £8.3m—a case that bears strikingly similar traits to the £60m heist in London. Both robberies involved heavy-duty drills to penetrate concrete walls, burglars disguised as workmen, timing over bank holidays, and the possibility of inside assistance, though the perpetrators of the Berlin raid have never been found.

# Summary

A toddler named Viktoria watched Disney's The Lion King for the first time, and her emotional reaction to Mufasa's death was captured on camera by her father, Barles Charkley. As the mournful scene progressed, viewers could see her bottom lip tremble, brush away tears, and gather her blanket around her shoulders in despair. The video, which Mr. Charkley posted to Facebook, has garnered over four million views and shows the powerful impact of this iconic Disney moment on young viewers.

# Summary

A couple in their 40s became unusual victims of age discrimination when HSBC rejected their £250,000 mortgage application, deeming the husband too old as he would exceed 65 when the 18-year loan matured. The Financial Ombudsman Service ordered HSBC to pay the couple £500 compensation after finding the bank 'relied on untested assumptions, stereotypes or generalisations in respect of age' in its unfair application of its age policy. The ruling, the first of its kind specifically on age discrimination for a mortgage, marks a significant development for borrowers struggling against strict age-related lending criteria.

# Summary

A Texas veterinarian, Dr. Kristen Lindsey, faced severe public backlash after posting a photo on Facebook showing herself with an orange tabby cat she had shot with an arrow, boasting about her "first bow kill." What began as a small social media post quickly spiraled into real-life consequences, including her firing from Washington County Animal Clinic and an ongoing police investigation, after animal rescuers suggested the cat was actually a pet belonging to an elderly couple rather than a feral animal. The incident sparked widespread outrage, with animal rights activists pushing for criminal charges and offering a reward for evidence leading to her arrest and conviction.

# Summary

Professor Ninian Peckitt, a world-renowned facial surgeon formerly of Doncaster, is accused of repeatedly punching a patient in the face to correct a fracture caused by an industrial accident, though he claims he only "digitally manipulated" the patient's face as part of a standard closed reduction procedure. The Medical Practitioners Tribunal Service hearing in Manchester was told that witnesses confirmed he actually punched the patient, and an expert report stated there is no medical basis for reducing a fracture using external force alone. Professor Peckitt faces being struck off the medical register following these allegations and similar accusations involving two other patients.

# Summary

Ben Powers, best known for playing Keith, the husband of main character Thelma (played by Bern Nadette Stanis) on the CBS sitcom Good Times, died on April 6 at the age of 64 at his home in New Bedford, Massachusetts. His on-screen wife Bern Nadette Stanis posted an emotional message on Facebook on Thursday to mark his death, though the cause has not been revealed by his family. Powers is survived by his mother and two sisters.

# Derby Rescue Point Against Brentford

Darren Bent scored a dramatic last-minute equaliser in stoppage time to secure a 1-1 draw for Derby against Brentford at the iPro Stadium. Brentford dominated large periods of the match and took a half-time lead through Alex Pritchard's brilliant free-kick in the 22nd minute, while also squandering several second-half chances including a shot cleared off the line by Craig Forsyth. Derby's equaliser came when Jeff Hendrick fired in a low shot in the second minute of stoppage time, allowing Bent to pounce and grab his 10th goal for the Rams, though the goal was considered harsh on the visitors who had controlled much of the play.

# Summary

A gang of four armed robbers used pink umbrellas to conceal their identities as they carried out a two-month crime spree, targeting 13 shops including supermarkets and convenience stores across the West Midlands and stealing £145,000 in cash, £2,000 worth of cigarettes, and cars valued at £30,000. The thieves threatened shop workers with hammers, knives, and screwdrivers during the raids, with CCTV footage capturing them using umbrellas to hide their faces as they entered premises. Following forensic evidence and mobile phone analysis, police arrested all four suspects, with Evans, Bell, Richardson, and Morgan receiving sentences totaling 27 years for conspiracy to rob.

# Summary

Italian explorer Alex Bellini plans to spend a year living inside a ball-shaped capsule on top of an iceberg in Greenland, seeking funding for his ambitious project called Adrift. While the capsule will expose him to the iceberg's dangers as it melts, cracks, and potentially flips, it is designed to keep him safe with features like an interior that rotates on roller balls and sensors to detect tremors. The aim of this extreme stunt is to highlight the effects of global warming by observing an iceberg in its final phase of life and raising awareness about how climate change melts icebergs worldwide and causes sea levels to rise.

# Summary

A photograph circulating on Snapchat, allegedly taken by a student at Geelong College, showed principal Andrew Barr viewing pornography on his office computer during work hours. After the image appeared on social media and the school conducted an investigation that included searching his computer, Mr. Barr resigned from his position, with the college council confirming the incident was a serious breach of their standards, though no laws were broken.

# Summary

A man named Francis Pusok, who was beaten by police over 80 times during his arrest on Thursday, has spoken out just a day after being released from jail to detail his painful experience. Helicopter cameras caught a swarm of officers kicking and punching the 30-year-old more than 80 times after he fled his southern California home when deputies attempted to arrest him on identity theft charges. Pusok claims he was handcuffed within 15 seconds but the officers continued to assault him while he lay helpless on the ground, and ten officers have since been placed on paid administrative leave while the FBI investigates the incident for excessive force.

# Summary

According to reports, the extremist Islamist group Hizb ut-Tahrir (HT), which has a significant UK support base, has allegedly joined forces with al-Qaeda's Nusra Front in Syria and attacked civil activists in Aleppo. Several British men linked to ISIS, including Mohammed 'Jihadi John' Emwazi, came into contact with HT while studying at UK universities. The article also covers controversy surrounding Liberal Democrat Parliamentary candidate Maajid Nawaz, a former HT member, who was filmed attempting to touch a lap dancer at a strip club, contradicting his public image as a feminist and family man.

# Summary

Marks & Spencer is introducing a new tropical hybrid fruit to UK shoppers called the "plango," a cross between a mango and a plum (Bouea macrophylla or mango plum) that has been developed through years of work with specialist growers in Thailand. The fruit features a bright orange edible skin, a sweet mango-like flavor, and a softer texture than traditional mangos, making it easy to eat without preparation. The plango will go on sale at Marks & Spencer this weekend, joining other unusual fruit hybrids already available on the market.

# Summary

The University of Cape Town's council has ordered the removal of a bronze statue of controversial British colonialist Cecil Rhodes, which was defaced and vandalized after protests from black students who found it a symbol of historical white oppression. The defaced statue has been tied to a crane for removal, while the government's arts and culture ministry spokesman said they are working to take an 'official position' on statues and other colonial symbols. However, the decision has sparked counter-protests from white Afrikaner solidarity groups seeking to protect their heritage, though it has not yet been decided where the statue will be permanently relocated.

# Summary

Hannah Bluck, an 18-year-old teenage Welsh international and football prodigy from Porthcawl, south Wales, has had her promising career halted for two years after being diagnosed with avascular necrosis, a rare condition in which her shin bone has died from lack of oxygen. Doctors warned her that her leg could collapse into her ankle if she resumes playing, ruling her out of the game indefinitely. Despite this setback, Hannah continues to dream of earning a full senior Wales cap and becoming a professional player, and has recently secured an appointment with a leading specialist in London for potential treatment options.

# Summary

Edinson Cavani has dismissed reports of a breakdown in his relationship with PSG teammate Zlatan Ibrahimovic, insisting they have a respectful partnership despite not being close friends. His main frustration centers on being played out of his favored striker position, which he says has contributed to a scoring drought in Ligue 1, though he expressed satisfaction with everything else at the club following PSG's 3-2 victory against Marseille on Sunday. Despite being linked with a summer exit, Cavani indicated his intention to honor his three-year contract, though he refused to completely rule out a possible move.

# England U-17 Squad Named for UEFA European Championship

England coach John Peacock has named his squad for next month's UEFA European Under-17 Championship in Bulgaria, with the Young Lions aiming to defend their title after winning on penalties last year. The team qualified with a perfect record and has been placed in Group D alongside Holland, Italy, and the Republic of Ireland, opening their campaign against Italy on May 7. The squad includes talented young players from Premier League clubs, with several earning recalls after missing the Elite Round qualifiers.

# Summary

Fireball Cinnamon Whisky, a relatively new spirit served at beachfront bars like Clayton's in South Padre Island, Texas, has become the fastest-growing major liquor brand in America, with bartender Casey Belue reporting they go through three to four bottles on an average night. The drink, which originated as Dr. McGillicuddy's Fireball Whisky before being rebranded by Sazerac in 2006, has skyrocketed from $2 million in retail sales in 2011 to $130 million by the following year, now ranking as the sixth-most popular liquor brand in the U.S. Its success is largely attributed to aggressive marketing through social media and a brand ambassador strategy that targeted college towns and vibrant bar scenes, though its popularity is being challenged by copycat products and concerns about its safety.

# Summary

Researchers at the University of Montana have claimed that eating fast food after a workout may be just as beneficial as dietary supplements. The study found that during recovery periods between exercise sessions, it didn't matter what food was eaten, as performance remained the same and glycogen levels (used by muscles as fuel) were actually slightly higher in the fast food group. However, researchers cautioned that the study involved a small sample size and the long-term effects of relying on fast food for workout recovery compared to healthier options remain unknown.

# Summary

Gael Monfils, the world No. 18 Frenchman who is sometimes fantastic but often frustrating, brought all the good parts of his game to bear in a thrilling last-16 encounter against Roger Federer at the Monte Carlo Masters, winning 6-4, 7-6 and advancing to his first quarter-final in Monaco. The 28-year-old also played with focus and commitment—attributes that can sometimes be found wanting in his game—displaying breathtaking athleticism and superior shotmaking throughout the match. The victory showcases just how good Monfils could have been and perhaps how good he still can be, as he moves on to face Grigor Dimitrov in an explosive quarter-final.

# Summary

Rev. Al Sharpton visited North Charleston, South Carolina on Sunday to address the Walter Scott situation at Charity Missionary Baptist Church, where elected officials and police listened as he discussed the need for national policing reform and praised Mayor Keith Summey for firing officer Michael Slager. Slager, 33, has been charged with murder for fatally shooting the unarmed Scott after a traffic stop, and hundreds of mourners attended Scott's burial service in Summerville on Saturday, where Scott's mother Judy Scott was seen wiping her eyes at her son's casket. Sharpton was also expected to attend a vigil near the scene of the shooting and denied reports that Scott's family had barred him from attending the funeral, explaining he was in New York for his National Action Network convention.

# Summary

Stewart Brand, editor of the Whole Earth Catalogue, argues that Earth's biodiversity is actually increasing despite widespread warnings from conservationists that the planet faces a sixth mass extinction rivaling the dinosaurs' extinction. Instead of focusing on extinction threats, Brand contends it is highly unlikely that such a mass extinction is occurring, pointing out that the rate of new species discovery is outstripping species loss. He believes conservation efforts are succeeding, with many threatened species recovering and damaged ecosystems being restored.

# Summary

CNN reports on ISIS documents obtained by researcher Aymenn Al-Tamimi that reveal the organization operates as a functioning government with official records including birth certificates, vaccination schedules, and policies on matters like rent disputes in territories it controls. Despite being known for brutal violence, ISIS attempts to portray itself as a legitimate authority by establishing bureaucratic systems, reopening universities with altered curricula, providing healthcare services, and justifying its laws through Islamic religious texts and jurisprudence. The group's governance strategy combines elements of stability and social services with strict religious enforcement to win over local populations and maintain absolute control over its territory.

# Summary

In Milwaukee, Wisconsin, Archie Brown Jr. struck and killed a 2-year-old child who ran in front of his van on Sunday evening. At the scene of the accident, an unidentified gunman opened fire, shooting and killing Brown and a 15-year-old boy, with police suggesting the shooting may have been an act of revenge for the child's death. Brown, a father of four and real estate entrepreneur, remained at the scene and cooperated with police before being shot in the head; the gunman remains at large.

# Royal Baby Betting Odds Tumble After Punter Bets £2,000 on Girl

Betting odds on the new royal baby being a girl have tumbled after 90 per cent of bets placed have been in favour of a female child, with a Scottish punter from Edinburgh placing a whopping £2,000 wager on a princess with Ladbrokes. Popular name choices for the new royal baby include Alice (3/1) for a girl and James (8/1) for a boy, with other royal-inspired names like Victoria (10/1), Arthur (10/1), and Charlotte (6/1) also in the running. Betting on the new royal baby's date of birth has also intensified, with April 18th, 19th and 20th proving most popular even though the official due date is April 25th.

# Summary

National sweethearts and television's golden girls Holly Willoughby, 34, and Davina McCall, 47, have joined forces in their first campaign together for Garnier Nutrisse, where they show off their glossy locks in the shoot. Meanwhile, Davina reaffirmed her commitment to her spouse of 15 years by renewing their wedding vows at the Little White Wedding Chapel in Las Vegas, with their three children writing the vows for the ceremony. The couple has since embarked on a road trip around California, visiting the picturesque coastal town of Carmel.

# Summary

A New York judge has ruled that Facebook is an acceptable method for serving divorce papers, allowing Brooklyn woman Ellanora Arthur Baidoo to serve her husband Victor Sena Blood-Dzraku via the social media platform after years of trying to locate him. According to Baidoo's attorney, Andrew Spinnell, the couple has been unable to find Blood-Dzraku to serve him the divorce summons because he claims to have no fixed address or employment, though Baidoo has been able to reach him by phone. Justice Matthew Cooper determined that social media represents the "next frontier" for delivering legal documents, and if Blood-Dzraku does not respond to the Facebook service, Baidoo can proceed with a default divorce.

# Turin Shroud on Public Display

The Turin Shroud, a piece of linen that devotees believe to be the burial cloth of Jesus Christ, went on public display on Sunday for the first time in five years, with more than one million people having already booked slots to see it until June 24. As one visitor noted, "Whether you believe or don't believe, there is no doubt it is something special." The 14-foot-long cloth, imprinted with the image of a crucified man, remains one of Christianity's most debated relics, with scientific dating suggesting it is a medieval forgery, though researchers have been unable to definitively establish how the image was created.

# Summary

A newly-built mansion on central London's Farm Street in Mayfair has gone on the market for £25 million, replacing a structure that once served as a milking parlour one hundred and fifty years ago. The four-bedroom property features all the trappings of modern luxury, including an indoor swimming pool, gym, cinema room, roof terrace, and a private lift. After developers demolished the original building in 2011, they constructed this 8,139 square foot contemporary home on the site, creating a striking contrast between Mayfair's historic agricultural past and its opulent present.

# Poldark Renewed for Second Series

As the final episodes of the BBC period drama Poldark approach, TV chiefs have announced the Cornwall-set series will return for eight more episodes, disappointing those seeking a break from the national obsession with heartthrob Aidan Turner. The renewal comes after the show helped BBC1 deliver its strongest start to a year in a decade, with an average of 8.1 million viewers per episode. The second series will continue the story based on Winston Graham's novels, following Ross Poldark and his wife Demelza through new adventures involving their child Jeremy and the scheming banker George Warleggan.

# Summary

According to a report from the Centers for Disease Control (CDC), gastrointestinal illness has affected 100 people aboard the cruise ship Celebrity Infinity, including 95 passengers and 5 crew members who have experienced vomiting, diarrhea, and other symptoms. The CDC has not yet determined the cause of the outbreak and has scheduled two staffers to meet the ship in San Diego on Monday to investigate further. This marks the third gastrointestinal illness outbreak on the Celebrity Infinity, with previous incidents occurring in 2006 and 2013.

# Summary

UltraHaptics, a young company featured on CNN, has a big dream of changing how we interact with electronic devices by using ultrasonic speakers to create tactile three-dimensional shapes in thin air, concentrating sound waves to a specific point that emits enough force to displace skin and create invisible but tangible interfaces. The technology has already generated strong interest, with UltraHaptics working with 15 to 20 clients from consumer electronics, home appliance, gaming companies, and the automotive industry, who are keen to incorporate this tactile ultrasound into their products. The company aims to have their first product featuring this innovative technology on shelves within a year, potentially revolutionizing everything from virtual reality to car dashboards and smartphones.

# Summary

Jodie Barden, 28, is forced to arrange the funerals for both her daughters, Ella (eight) and Chloe (one), as neither will make adulthood due to Cockayne Syndrome, an incredibly rare genetic disorder characterized by premature aging, an underdeveloped nervous system, and a small head. Both girls suffer from this condition and have a life expectancy of just 12 years. Despite this heartbreaking prognosis, Mrs. Barden and her family are dedicated to making memorable experiences for their daughters while they are alive, having raised approximately £14,000 for Cockayne Syndrome charities through various fundraising events.

# Summary

Meet 11-year-old Damon Muller from Queensland, who was diagnosed with Juvenile Dermatomyositis (JDM), a rare autoimmune disease that means he can't go out in the sun without developing a severe rash and risks his throat swelling so much it almost closes. Since his diagnosis in November, Damon has almost doubled in size due to swelling and bloating caused by high-dose steroid treatment, resulting in severe stretch marks on his thighs and a very round tummy. The disease has severely limited his mobility and outdoor activities, forcing him to stay indoors most of the time, while his family seeks support and information through a U.S. foundation conference.

# Summary

Chelsy Davy, 29, debuted a glamorous and sophisticated style at the launch party for London restaurant The Ivy Chelsea Garden, where she was seen laughing and joking with friend Irene Forte. The blonde wore a white layered chiffon top paired with flattering navy trousers featuring zip-detail on the ankles, which she accessorized with tan woven ankle-strap heels and a croc-print clutch bag. The outfit showcased Davy's shift toward a more refined approach to fashion as she approaches her thirties, a departure from her previously more casual style.

# Summary

Hundreds of mourners attended the funeral of nine-year-old Chloe in northern France today, where huge screens were erected outside the packed church to accommodate those who could not fit inside. The tragic killing of Chloe sparked a national outpouring of grief across France after her body was discovered in a migrant camp last week, having been raped and murdered by Polish migrant Zbigniew Huminski. The case has shocked the country and prompted thousands to participate in a "White March" tribute to the young schoolgirl.

# Summary

A lack of iron, especially in women, is known to trigger hair loss, and while Dr Jolliffe says there is published evidence both for and against treating it with iron supplements, she has observed in her practice that correcting low ferritin levels does stop hair shedding. Eating sufficient protein is also vital for healthy hair, though experts debate whether it needs to be consumed at every meal, and dietary changes typically take at least six months to show visible results. While various supplements like Viviscal and caffeine treatments claim to promote hair growth, Professor Messenger notes that few have strong clinical evidence to support their effectiveness in people.

# Summary

Suma, a 36-year-old orangutan at Melbourne Zoo, suffers from arthritis, a condition she was first diagnosed with in 2013 and initially managed with daily medication to relieve pain. On Thursday, surgeons captured photographs of Suma undergoing a thorough health examination, during which orthopedic surgeon Marino Pirpiris administered a steroid injection to reduce her pain for months or even years. Although arthritis is not unusual for middle-aged orangutans like Suma, the examination also included X-rays, a full check-up of her ears, teeth, and eyes, plus blood and urine samples to monitor her overall health.

# Summary

Louis Tomlinson from One Direction was photographed during a wild night of partying at London's Soho Hotel, appearing to roll a joint while seated next to a suspicious green substance. New Snapchat images emerged showing the 23-year-old singer partying with a group of female friends until at least 5:48am after leaving a nightclub at 4am. This incident comes just weeks after bandmate Zayn Malik quit the group and follows a previous controversial video from 2014 in which Louis and Zayn were seen smoking what appeared to be marijuana.

# Summary

Century-old photographs taken between 1900 and 1911 during China's Qing dynasty offer rare glimpses of Beijing's famous landmarks during a time when they had barely any foreign visitors and no one had ever heard of a selfie. These early holiday snaps of the Chinese capital show the city's most iconic attractions, such as the Temple of Heaven and Summer Palace, almost completely devoid of crowds—a stark contrast to modern-day Beijing, which attracts millions of visitors annually and has become so popular that the Forbidden City announced it would limit visitor numbers, with Chinese state media reporting that the former imperial palace alone attracts over 14 million visitors each year.

# Summary

A Pain and Gain report released by CoreLogic for the December quarter has revealed that home resales recording a gross loss were at an all-time low, with only 8.6 percent of homes recording losses compared to their prior purchase price across the country. With many homes under the hammer this weekend, Australian capital cities can expect record-breaking profits, with Sydney recording the lowest loss-making resales at just 2.4 percent, while 32.3 percent of properties sold for more than double their original price. The total value of profit-making resales exceeded $17 billion—over three and a half times more than the $418 million in losses—demonstrating a strong market for property sellers in capital cities.

# Summary

West Bromwich Albion manager Tony Pulis produced a gritty 2-0 victory over his former club Crystal Palace, with James Morrison and Craig Gardner scoring to secure the win despite Palace's dominance, which was met by West Brom's stubborn doggedness. The 57-year-old Pulis claimed he will never change his distinctive touchline attire of cap, track suit, and white trainers, explaining that he wants to be close to the action rather than sit in a suit upstairs. The three points moved West Brom to 36 points, virtually guaranteeing their Premier League survival, demonstrating that Pulis's gritty style works at every game beneath the peak of his cap.

# Summary

Crystal Palace co-chairman Steve Parish celebrated his club's 2-1 victory over champions Manchester City by posting an Instagram picture with Palace manager Alan Pardew, England boss Roy Hodgson, and Bill Wyman, one of the original members of The Rolling Stones. Wyman, an avid Palace supporter for 68 years, was honored in the Palace boardroom despite Parish's normally strict dress code, as the legendary musician has attended games since age 10 and demonstrated genuine passion for the club and the game. The victory was particularly significant as it showcased seven English players in Palace's lineup, with England manager Roy Hodgson able to observe numerous domestic talent during the match.

# Summary

The Solar Impulse 2, an experimental plane attempting to fly around the world without using fuel, has been grounded by weather in Chongqing, China, turning what was supposed to be an overnight pit stop into a two-and-a-half week unexpected layover that is testing the patience of pilots Bertrand Piccard and Andre Borschberg and their team of over 60 members. The solar-powered aircraft, which relies on near-perfect weather conditions to fly, is only a quarter of the way through its 35,000-kilometer journey across five months. Despite the delay, the team remains focused on their mission to prove the power of renewable energy, with the Pacific Ocean crossing considered the critical next phase of their journey.

# Summary

Fresh from his Masters triumph, Jordan Spieth visited the Empire State Building's observation deck on Monday while proudly wearing his green jacket, celebrating his four-stroke victory at Augusta. Spieth broke several scoring records on his way to victory, including equalling Tiger Woods' 18-under total from 1997 and becoming the championship's second-youngest winner of all time. The 21-year-old golfer, who is now ranked world No. 2, attributed his success to the disappointment of last year's loss and his determination to play his favorite course with focus and determination.

# Summary

The article examines politicians' living quarters to determine how relatable they are to ordinary people, with particular focus on Prime Minister David Cameron's kitchen, which has attracted attention partly because he is known as a "toff" (upper-class person) and Britain remains obsessed with class distinctions. An etiquette expert provides a humorous "household audit" that scores homes based on possession of items deemed prestigious—such as cafetières, Agas, proper tea cups, and fabric sofas—to determine whether one's house qualifies as upper class, upper-middle class, middle-middle class, or lower-middle class. The checklist satirizes British class consciousness by suggesting that seemingly minor household choices reveal a person's true social standing.

# Wedding Summary

Andy Murray married Kim Sears on Saturday at Dunblane Cathedral in a service conducted by Reverend Colin Renwick before family members and close friends, while hundreds of well-wishers gathered outside to greet the couple. Kim showed her delight as the happy pair made their way out of the cathedral covered in confetti, with one of the biggest cheers reserved for Andy's mother Judy Murray, who arrived looking elegant in a white overcoat and gold dress. The wedding drew massive media attention and community support, with the reception held at the 15-bedroom Cromlix House Hotel that Andy had purchased two years earlier.

# Summary

Alastair Cook has been working on a new, more open stance with former England batting coach Graham Gooch over the past few months, but appears to have overdone it during the first Test against the West Indies. Comparing his stance from a 2007 match at Lord's—when he was playing well with a slightly open front foot—to his recent performance, his front foot is now positioned far too open, forcing him to play shots with his hands away from his body and making him vulnerable to dismissal. Although Cook's technique looks exaggerated in action photos, the article notes his mental strength as his greatest asset, and encourages confidence in his ability to regain form once he settles into his innings.

# Summary

Brazilian right-back Dani Alves has rejected Barcelona's final contract offer and will leave the club this summer after seven successful years, during which he won four Spanish titles and the Champions League twice. Alves's agent and ex-wife, Dinorah Santana, said at a press conference on Thursday that her client had rejected a three-year contract extension that was dependent on the player taking part in 60 per cent of matches. The departure is expected to attract interest from top clubs including Manchester United, Manchester City, and Paris St Germain, with Santana confirming that Alves currently has two three-year offers on the table.

# Summary

Robin Ellis, the original star of 1970s Poldark who appeared in a cameo on the BBC remake concluding this Sunday, was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes in 1999 at age 58, despite being slim his entire life with a perfect physique that many associate with overweight people. Although he had none of the typical symptoms and lived a healthy lifestyle, Robin discovered that type 2 diabetes can affect apparently slim individuals due to genetic factors and visceral fat hidden in the abdomen. Through significant lifestyle changes including eliminating refined carbohydrates and walking regularly, Robin managed to control his blood sugar for ten years without medication, and now advocates for taking diabetes seriously while remaining optimistic about his future.

# Summary

Former New England Patriots tight end Aaron Hernandez was moved to Souza-Baranowski Correctional Center, a maximum-security prison in Shirley, Massachusetts, one week after being convicted of first-degree murder on April 15 and sentenced to life in prison. Hernandez was transferred from Cedar Junction prison in Walpole, where he had been held since his conviction for killing 27-year-old Odin Lloyd in 2013, who was dating the sister of Hernandez's fiancée. Souza-Baranowski, the state's most advanced prison facility located about 40 miles west of Boston, houses over 1,000 inmates and will now hold Hernandez as its most famous inmate.

# Summary

Jamie Robbins handed himself into police a year after raiding the Select 'n' Save convenience store in Kings Norton, Birmingham on January 13, 2014, stealing £1,600 with an accomplice. The unsolved case was only resolved when Robbins walked into a police station saying he preferred life in jail, providing full details of the robbery. He was sentenced to four-and-a-half years in prison after pleading guilty to robbery, though his preference for jail time changed when he learned his girlfriend was pregnant the day before his court appearance.

# Summary

The world's first cloned camel, Injaz, is now pregnant after being cloned from ovarian cells in 2009 and born from a surrogate mother in the desert sheikdoms of the Persian Gulf, proving that cloned camels can reproduce naturally like valued commodities used for racing, transport, and beauty contests that can make owners millions of dollars. Since the first mammal, Dolly the sheep, was cloned 19 years ago in Edinburgh, scientists have successfully cloned various animals including mice, cows, pigs, and dogs; however, recent studies on genetically modified human embryos have sparked fear among many scientists that the technology could allow parents to select genes passed to future generations, though others argue it could eliminate inherited diseases like cystic fibrosis, and such work is already illegal in many countries like the UK but is permitted in the US and China where modifications to sperm and eggs can be preserved in the germline.

# Summary

Sir Alex Ferguson, the most successful manager in British football history, spoke with snooker champion John Parrott and presenter Hazel Irvine during BBC's coverage of the World Championship at the Crucible, where he explained why he rates Cristiano Ronaldo above Lionel Messi as the world's greatest player. Ferguson argued that Ronaldo could score for any team due to his versatility, speed, and athleticism, while he believes Messi is primarily a Barcelona player. The 73-year-old, who has always been close to the Portuguese superstar from their time together at Manchester United, discussed this preference while playing a frame of snooker with Parrott.

# Summary

On Saturday night, the Fenerbahce team bus carrying players and coaching staff was shot at while returning from an away game, with the driver appearing to have been hit by a bullet (though initially it was uncertain whether he was hit by a stone or a gunshot). Security officers on board quickly took control of the bus and safely stopped it after the driver was hit, averting a potential accident. Though the bus was attacked with what appeared to be a shotgun or rifle, none of the players or officials were injured, though the driver suffered a head wound and was hospitalized.

# Summary

Nicola Sturgeon, Scotland's First Minister, impressed a Britain-wide television audience with her self-assured and poised performance in stilettoes and a new hairstyle, addressing millions of Britons rather than just the usual five million Scots. Judy Murray, mother of tennis star Andy Murray (who had voiced support for Scottish independence on the eve of last year's referendum), tweeted about Sturgeon's triumph, saying "GSM Sturgeon" (Game, Set and Match). An instant YouGov poll found that 28 percent of viewers thought Sturgeon was the best performer, marking a significant moment of national exposure for Scotland's First Minister.

# Summary

Ulster and Ireland prop Declan Fitzpatrick, 31, has retired from rugby on medical grounds after experiencing multiple concussive episodes in recent seasons that were progressively slower to resolve. Following consultation with a neurologist and medical tests, Fitzpatrick was advised by medical professionals that retiring would be in his best long-term interests. Fitzpatrick, who earned seven caps for Ireland and made 98 appearances for Ulster over nine seasons, expressed gratitude to the medical teams while confirming that his symptoms continue to improve.

# Summary

FLIR Systems Inc., an Oregon-based defense contractor, agreed to pay $9.5 million to settle bribery charges filed by the Securities and Exchange Commission on Wednesday for giving expensive gifts and arranging luxury trips for Middle Eastern government officials. The SEC stated that two FLIR employees in Dubai gave luxury watches to five Saudi Arabia Ministry of Interior officials in 2009 and arranged a 20-night international trip, which influenced more than $7 million in sales profits. Additionally, the company must report to the SEC on its compliance efforts for two years, and the two former employees involved also agreed to settle charges separately.

# Summary

With anticipation ramped up ahead of the $300 million mega fight between Floyd Mayweather Jr. and Manny Pacquiao on May 2 in Las Vegas, American television network Showtime has released a promotional short film taking viewers inside Mayweather's training gym. The dramatic video showcases the undefeated American dedicating himself to the sport and pushing himself to the limit as he prepares for the biggest fight the sport has ever witnessed. Mayweather, already within three-and-a-half pounds of the welterweight limit, has expressed that he has never wanted to win a fight so badly in his life.

# Summary

A heroic 58-year-old mother, Diwalinen Vankar, saved her 19-year-old daughter Kanta from a 13-foot crocodile on the banks of the Vishwamitri River, 40 kilometres from Vadodara, India, by repeatedly hitting the creature with a wooden washing paddle during a 10-minute battle. When the crocodile grabbed Kanta's right leg and dragged her into the murky river, her mother initially tried pulling her free by hand, but the crocodile's powerful jaws would not let her go until Vankar grabbed the paddle and struck its head forcefully. Kanta survived the attack with leg injuries and was treated at a hospital, while wildlife officials warned villagers to avoid the river, which is home to over 200 crocodiles.

# Summary

Ahead of the 79th Masters, Sportsmail counts down the 20 greatest shots ever seen at Augusta National, with Mark O'Meara's 1998 victory topping the first portion of the list. At age 41, O'Meara shot two birdies in three holes from the 15th on Sunday and made a good approach into the 18th, leaving him with a 20-footer to beat David Duval and Fred Couples, which he drained to win the tournament. After his victory, O'Meara was handed his green jacket by his great friend Tiger Woods in the Butler Cabin.

# Summary

On tonight's episode of Married at First Sight, all three couples embark on their honeymoons but quickly encounter serious trouble in paradise. Jaclyn Methuen, who nearly left the altar because she deemed Ryan Ranellone unattractive, continues to put her new husband in the friend zone during their Puerto Rico trip and admits she wanted to be a runaway bride. Meanwhile, the other two couples also struggle with relationship issues, including arguments over insensitive comments and lack of family support during their honeymoon getaways.

# Summary

The Taliban recently published an 11-page biography of Mullah Mohammed Omar, the reclusive founder of the Taliban, with the primary message that he remains the leader of their Islamic Emirate despite years of speculation about his death. The biography, released by the Taliban's Cultural Commission, appears designed to dispel rumors of Omar's demise and reassert his leadership credentials at a time when ISIS leader Abu Bakr al-Baghdadi has declared himself caliph. The document describes Omar's daily work schedule and early life, though it omits mention of the Taliban's alliance with bin Laden and al Qaeda, as well as U.S. support for the mujahedeen forces that Omar initially fought with against Soviet invaders.

# Deflategate Summary

While the NFL draft begins on April 30 with the Tampa Bay Buccaneers on the clock, the clock is still ticking for the New England Patriots as they await the results of the "Deflategate" investigation, which has been ongoing for more than three months since the NFL launched it in January to determine why 11 of 12 Patriots game balls were underinflated during the AFC Championship game. Led by attorney Ted Wells, the investigation's completion date remains unclear, though if wrongdoing is found, the Patriots could face fines and/or the loss of a draft pick. CNN attempted to reach Wells and the NFL for an update but has not received a response, leaving the outcome and timeline still uncertain.

# Summary

Bianca Gascoigne, 28-year-old daughter of former England midfielder Paul Gascoigne, was mugged by a gang of eight on bikes in central London on Tuesday afternoon while walking in Marylebone. The attackers stole her mobile phone, which reportedly contained private texts from her father, and attempted to snatch her bag before fleeing when she fought back. Bianca took to Twitter to brand the muggers "lowlifes on bikes," visibly upset by the theft of her phone and its private contents.

# Summary

Adam Rooney is determined to prevent Aberdeen's outstanding season from ending on a sour note by stopping Celtic from clinching their fourth successive Premiership title at Pittodrie on May 11. Despite sticking with Celtic throughout the season, Aberdeen currently trail by eight points, and Rooney admits it would be painful to watch their rivals celebrate the league victory on their home patch. The 27-year-old striker, who has scored 27 goals this season, is confident that Aberdeen can remain competitive and close the gap to Celtic in the coming years if they continue to strengthen the squad.

# Summary

Former rap mogul Marion "Suge" Knight was ordered Thursday in Los Angeles to stand trial for murder, attempted murder, and hit-and-run charges stemming from a deadly confrontation on a movie set in January 2015, with his bail lowered to $10 million. Deputy District Attorney Cynthia Barnes argued that Knight used his car as a deadly weapon with premeditation and intent, losing any right to self-defense when he ran over the victim a second time. Knight is scheduled to be arraigned on April 30 and faces up to life in prison if convicted, marking the latest run-in with the law for the former Death Row Records founder who previously was connected to Tupac Shakur's death in 1996.

# Summary

Hillary Clinton's Iowa campaign staged a seemingly spontaneous coffee shop "roundtable" that was actually orchestrated by campaign staff, who recruited Democratic Party insiders including Austin Bird—a hospital government-affairs staffer who had previously driven Vice President Joe Biden's limousine during a campaign stop for Democrat Bruce Braley—rather than typical voters. Bird and two other Democratic Party activists were invited to what they thought was a strategy meeting before being driven to the coffee shop to meet Clinton, with news outlets misrepresenting Bird as a St. Ambrose University student rather than a political operative. The staged event is part of Clinton's carefully coordinated Iowa campaign strategy to project a spontaneous, populist image while facing criticism for manufacturing grassroots appearances.

# Summary

An HIV self-testing kit has launched for sale online in the UK for the first time, using a small amount of blood from a finger-prick sample to detect HIV antibodies and provide results in just 15 minutes, helping address the fact that an estimated 26,000 people in the UK have HIV without knowing it and may be transmitting the disease to others. With the National AIDS Trust warning that 40 percent of those living with HIV remain undiagnosed for at least four years and are 11 times more likely to die in the first year after late diagnosis, BioSure founder Brigette Bard emphasized that "knowing your HIV status is critical" and that the launch of this product will empower people to test themselves discreetly and conveniently, significantly improving their health outcomes. Positive results must be confirmed by a healthcare professional, and those in high-risk groups are recommended to test every three months.

# Summary

California's Level IV maximum-security prisons house approximately 162,000 inmates, with an estimated 97 percent gang-affiliated, where gangs rule life much the same way as on the streets. However, for those who choose to leave the criminal life behind, the California Department of Corrections has established Sensitive Needs Housing Units that offer inmates a chance at a different way of living inside prison walls. These specialized units have seen a dramatic increase in requests since 1999 from former gang members seeking protection, and they provide safer environments where inmates of rival gangs and races can coexist peacefully, in stark contrast to the violence and segregation that characterizes regular prison life.

# Summary

Direct Assist, a personal injury claims company based in Lancashire, has collapsed after being fined £80,000 by the ICO for making nuisance calls to households registered with the Telephone Preference Service. Despite 525 warnings from the TPS and 801 complaints, the company deliberately continued calling people who had not given permission, with one elderly deaf complainant saying the calls left them in fear of answering their phone. The company instructed staff to use lists including TPS-registered numbers and told complainants they would likely be called for three years until they made a claim, showing a blatant disregard for privacy laws.

# Summary

Jose Mourinho has established himself as the best manager in Premier League history at shutting out opponents, with his Chelsea side maintaining 101 clean sheets in 189 matches—a 53.4 per cent rate that edges out his predecessor Rafa Benitez. A stern defensive line has twice helped Mourinho win the Premier League title with Chelsea, and a third trophy is likely upon the resolution of the 2014-15 campaign, as statistics demonstrate his superior defensive record and lowest average of goals conceded per game at 0.63.

# Summary

Authorities in Cape Town, South Africa detained a 15-year-old girl at the airport after receiving information that she was attempting to leave the country to join ISIS. According to State Security Minister David Mahlobo, the girl had been using social media platforms to interact with unknown individuals and consuming material suggesting interest in joining the terrorist group. Officials released the girl into her family's care after debriefing them, though the investigation into how she was recruited and how her travel was arranged is ongoing.

# Summary

Ben Powers, who played Keith Anderson, the husband of Thelma (BernNadette Stanis), on the final season of the classic CBS sitcom "Good Times," has died at age 64. Powers died on April 6 in New Bedford, Massachusetts, according to his family's announcement, though no cause of death was revealed. He joined the cast for the show's sixth and final season from 1978 to 1979, playing a professional football player whose character marries Thelma but injures his leg leaving the church.

# Summary

A 39-year-old man, reportedly known as Karl Kay on Facebook and a father of six, was fatally shot in an execution-style attack while sitting in his car in his mother's Altona Meadows driveway in Melbourne's southwest at approximately 2am on Sunday. The shooting, which left six children without a father, is believed to have been a targeted attack, and police have confirmed the victim was known to them. No arrests have been made, and police have not commented on whether the incident was gang-related.

# Summary

The author, an amateur wildlife photographer, crawled commando-style under an open-sided Land Rover and came face-to-face with a 4,500lb rhino and her crash of three others, needing to stay calm to take photographs; thankfully, the female rhino found him of no consequence and wandered off, after which he and his mother Kay Burley climbed back into the vehicle. During their luxury safari at Sir Richard Branson's Ulusaba Game Reserve in South Africa, they tracked various wildlife including lions, elephants, and ultimately discovered leopard cubs frolicking in a tree after a honey bird and an impala antelope's warning call led their tracker Danny to the magical sighting. His mother sat statue-still throughout the adventure, having adopted a nonchalant expression while experiencing close encounters with Africa's most impressive animals.

# Summary

After neighbors rejected his plans for a Lucasfilm studio on his California estate, George Lucas is now building an affordable housing complex to house 224 low-income families and seniors on the 52-acre project. Though Lucas's representatives insist the development is not retaliation, the move demonstrates the filmmaker's determination to do something beneficial with his land after the studio proposal was left untouched by the community. The $200 million project, submitted for planning this week, will be funded and designed by Lucas himself on his Grady Ranch estate in Marin County.

# Summary

Photographer Marc Latremouille captured stunning aerial photographs of a great grey owl with a five-foot wingspan swooping down on a tiny field mouse in the snowy fields of Ontario, Canada. The deadly predator, aided by its superior hearing and soft feathers that allow silent flight, stalked the rodent from treetops before launching its attack and scooping up the mouse with its claws. These bird's-eye-view images showcase the owl's impressive hunting skills during one of Latremouille's photography workshops.

# Summary

On May 11, 1985, a devastating fire at Bradford City's Valley Parade stadium killed 56 people, and a new book by survivor Martin Fletcher claims the fire was not an accident but rather linked to a sequence of eight other fires at businesses owned by then-chairman Stafford Heginbotham over the previous 18 years. Fletcher, who lost his father John and three other family members in the tragedy, spent 15 years researching the disaster and published his findings in the book "Fifty-Six – The Story of the Bradford Fire," serialized in The Guardian.
