In a stunning upset, crisis-hit Parma pulled off the biggest surprise of the season by defeating runaway Serie A leaders Juventus 1-0, inflicting only the second league defeat on Massimiliano Allegri's side, with Jose Mauri scoring the decisive goal and goalkeeper Antonio Mirante making crucial saves. Despite being declared bankrupt with unpaid players, Parma has strung together impressive results including a 1-0 victory over Udinese and a 1-1 draw at Inter Milan last weekend. Juventus, which rested several key players ahead of their Champions League quarter-final, failed to create meaningful scoring opportunities after going behind and remain 14 points clear at the top of the table.

# Summary

Photographer Simon Brann Thorpe created the "Toy Soldiers" project to combat the desensitization people experience from seeing images of war and conflict constantly plastered across TV screens and media outlets. He traveled to the desert of Western Sahara to photograph real soldiers from the Polisario Front posed as green plastic figurines, creating a visual metaphor that makes viewers question their emotional and political responses to warfare. The project aims to raise awareness about the long-disputed conflict in Western Sahara and challenge how audiences will consume war imagery in the future.

# Summary

A seven-year-old boy was raped and set on fire by two youths in Pagma Nagar, Bhiwandi (a suburb of Mumbai), while he was buying food. The boy suffered 30 percent burns to his body but managed to escape, according to Inspector Sasthe. Police have registered a case and are investigating the shocking incident.

# Summary

Jurgen Klopp confirmed on Wednesday that he will leave Borussia Dortmund at the end of the season after seven years in charge, putting England's top clubs on alert. Although bookmakers immediately installed him as the favorite to replace Manuel Pellegrini at Manchester City, it is understood that the struggling Premier League champions have no plans to make such a move. Klopp, who has been linked with several English teams including Liverpool and Arsenal in the past, stated he has not had contact with other clubs but is open to offers and will not take a sabbatical.

# Summary

Sean McCabe, a 30-year-old father-of-three from Wales, received devastating news in late 2013 that he had stage four Hodgkin Lymphoma with only two months to live, prompting him to organize his own funeral by choosing music and booking St David's Church. In an extraordinary turn of events, his cancer went into remission, allowing him to change his church reservation from a funeral to a wedding ceremony. He is now set to marry his longtime fiancée Lisa Williams on July 25, with his three children participating in the celebration of life.

# Summary

An eight-year-old boy died a day after his family's car plunged into Los Angeles Harbor in San Pedro on Thursday evening. His 13-year-old brother, identified as Abdelkrim Elmezayen, was found dead in the water shortly after 6pm, while their parents were rescued and are now in stable condition at the hospital. Nearly 100 emergency personnel, including firefighters and divers, responded to the scene at the port, though the cause of the accident remains under investigation.

# Summary

Qatar's investment arm has further tightened its grip on London's real estate by agreeing to buy a majority stake in the company that owns iconic five-star hotels including Claridge's, The Berkeley, and The Connaught from billionaire brothers Sir David and Sir Frederick Barclay. While the exact value of the deal is not known, some reports suggest it could be worth as much as £1.6 billion. This acquisition is the latest in a series of high-profile purchases as the Gulf state's investment arm continues snapping up a number of London's landmark buildings, including The Shard, Harrods, and Canary Wharf.

# Summary

A grey 2005 Toyota Kluger driven by Sudanese mother Akon Guode crashed into a lake at Wyndham Vale in Melbourne's outer west on Wednesday, killing three children: one-year-old Bol and four-year-old twins Madit and Anger, while a fifth child survived. After being released from police custody, the mother was defended by the children's father, Joseph Manyang, who insisted she was a "good mother" and innocent of any wrongdoing. Police are continuing their investigation into the incident to determine whether it was an accident or deliberate act, and are appealing to the Sudanese community for information about the family's circumstances.

# Summary

Scientists claim that computer keyboards could be a powerful new tool in their battle against Parkinson's disease, as researchers believe a person's keystrokes may reveal a huge amount of information about their motor skills. Researchers at MIT are working on creating a specialized keyboard that can tell doctors if someone has the neuron impairment simply by analyzing the way they type, particularly by measuring "key hold time" to detect motor function deficiencies. Preliminary results from studies of Parkinson's patients show promising potential, and the team is now planning larger studies while also developing smartphone apps to gather similar data from mobile devices.

# Summary

Liverpool manager Brendan Rodgers held a press conference to preview Saturday's match against Arsenal while addressing Raheem Sterling's contract situation following the young winger's BBC interview. Sterling, 20, revealed he is not ready to sign Liverpool's £100,000-a-week contract offer and admitted he was flattered by Arsenal's interest, an interview conducted without the club's prior permission. Rodgers insisted Sterling will not leave Liverpool this summer and emphasized the club's status as a "superpower" that does not need to sell its best players, though he acknowledged the unauthorized interview was a mistake by the young player.

# Summary

Mark Hughes has dismissed reports of Asmir Begovic's move to Real Madrid, expressing confidence that the goalkeeper will sign a new contract with Stoke this summer. The club is also hopeful of concluding the signing of teenage winger Moha El Ouriachi from Barcelona as they continue to develop their young squad. With 43 points from their current campaign and six games remaining, 10th-placed Stoke are aiming to better last season's record Premier League haul of 50 points, though they will be without several injured players including on-loan winger Victor Moses for Saturday's clash against Southampton.

# Summary

Aston Villa defeated Tottenham Hotspur 1-0 at White Hart Lane, with Christian Benteke scoring the opening goal in the 35th minute to give Villa a crucial advantage in their fight for Premier League survival. Benteke, who had scored a hat-trick in a 3-3 draw with Queens Park Rangers on Tuesday, continued his fine form and was mobbed by teammates after his header, pointing to the sky in celebration. Manager Tim Sherwood, who was famously sacked by Tottenham the previous summer, celebrated ecstatically at the final whistle, securing three precious points as his side moved closer to avoiding relegation.

# Summary

Gerard T. "The Frenchman" Ouimette, a former mob enforcer with ties to New England crime boss Raymond L.S. Patriarca, has died at age 75 in a federal prison in North Carolina after being recently diagnosed with lung cancer. According to a 1979 FBI document, Ouimette controlled a gangster network responsible for gambling, loansharking, extortion, and murder, and was suspected of involvement in up to eight murders, though he was never convicted of any homicides. Ouimette spent 46 years behind bars and became the first criminal in New England sentenced to life in prison under the federal "three strikes" provision in 1996 after his conviction for extortion, which followed more than 10 prior convictions including one for punching an FBI agent.

# Summary

Danny Cipriani has jetted off to Dubai for a short break after helping Sale Sharks to a 23-6 victory against Gloucester, where he contributed 13 points from the boot in a fine display. With Sale out of contention in Europe, Cipriani and his team-mates have taken advantage of a rare free weekend to recharge before their crucial final four rounds of Aviva Premiership action. The playmaker has been integral to Sale's improved form this season and will return to action on April 12 when Sale face London Irish.

# Summary

Owen Farrell, the England international fly-half, hopes to be in contention for Saracens' European Champions Cup semi-final against Clermont Auvergne on April 18, after being sidelined since suffering a knee ligament injury against the same opponents in January. His absence during the RBS 6 Nations allowed George Ford to excel in the number 10 shirt as England finished second. Farrell is optimistic about his recovery, stating that his knee is feeling good after recently returning to running, kicking, and tackling in training, with a final decision on his fitness to be made after this week's preparations.

# Summary

Officer Michael Slager, 33, is being held in isolation at Charleston County jail and cannot walk down a hallway without the entire cell block being cleared first, according to his attorney Andy Savage. Slager is charged with murder for shooting Walter Scott, 50, during a traffic stop over a broken tail light in North Charleston, South Carolina. A jail staff member indicated that Slager would not survive in the general prison population, as inmates are aware of his case and pose a threat to him.

# Summary

Tottenham manager Mauricio Pochettino has praised goalkeeper Michel Vorm's professionalism and character, expressing confidence that the Dutch keeper can perform well when he steps in for injured Hugo Lloris at Burnley on Sunday. Although Vorm was disappointed to be left on the bench for last month's Capital One Cup final defeat to Chelsea despite playing in earlier rounds, Pochettino believes the 31-year-old has the mentality to produce a solid performance in action. The manager acknowledged it was difficult to leave players out, but emphasized that Vorm knew when signing from Swansea that he would be a deputy behind Lloris, and remains a good professional ready for the challenge.

# Summary

An Uber customer named Jaime Hessel was infuriated when she received an eye-popping bill of $16,000 (with a $4,000 refund, totaling $12,251.49) for a 7-mile ride across New York after her driver was distracted, took an inefficient route, and drove dangerously, prompting her to demand to be let out early. After she complained about being overcharged, Uber initially offered only a $15 refund, but then mistakenly sent the massive bill, which she reported couldn't actually be charged because her credit card had expired. Feeling she can't trust the company with her new card details after nearly being charged the outrageous sum, Hessel said she's unlikely to use Uber again, though the company has since wiped the bill and apologized for the clerical error.

# Summary

Wanting to get online but lacking wifi coverage is a common problem, however Mother Nature recently appeared to lend a helping hand by giving locals in central China what could be the world's largest 'Wi-Fi cloud.' According to People's Daily Online, a cloud formation perfectly resembling the global Wi-Fi symbol—featuring one dot and three curved lines radiating from it—appeared in the sky above Xiangtan city in Hunan Province last week. The unusual phenomenon was photographed by a student and subsequently shared thousands of times on Chinese social media, with users joking about the cloud's incredible coverage and speculating about the password.

# Summary

Jack Wilshere, an Arsenal midfielder who works alongside players like Mesut Ozil, was left starstruck on Thursday evening when he met Brazil legend Pele at the launch party of 10Ten Talent. Wilshere was given the opportunity to interview the three-time World Cup winner, with Glenn Hoddle also in attendance as a fellow client of the talent agency. During the event, Pele reflected on Brazil's disappointing 7-1 World Cup semi-final loss to Germany, describing it as "a disaster," while praising Brazilian Premier League players like Oscar and Ramires for their quick recovery this season.

# Summary

Italy is coping with a rising wave of desperate migrants from Africa and the Middle East attempting to reach Europe, with 8,480 migrants rescued between Friday and Monday according to the Italian coast guard. Save the Children fears that 400 migrants may be missing after a ship carrying 550 people allegedly capsized in the Mediterranean Sea off the Libyan coast, though the Italian coast guard has not yet confirmed the incident. The surge in migrant crossings is attributed to overcrowded vessels operated by smugglers, poor weather conditions, and transit routes through Libya, with at least 480 migrants having died crossing the Mediterranean since the beginning of 2015.

# Summary

Saracens flanker Jacques Burger has been cited by European Professional Club Rugby for alleged foul play during the European Champions Cup quarter-final against Racing Metro in Paris, which Saracens won 12-11. Burger is accused of striking Racing scrum-half Maxime Machenaud midway through the match, a complaint made by match citing commissioner Eugene Ryan. The 31-year-old Namibia international will face a disciplinary hearing on Thursday, where he could face a ban ranging from two weeks to a year depending on the severity of the offense.

# Summary

Chile's Calbuco Volcano erupted on Thursday for the third time in a week, according to the National Service of Geology and Mining, with the explosion producing an extensive plume but proving smaller than the previous two eruptions. Gregorio Billikopf, who lives across Lake Llanquihue from the volcano and has been documenting all three eruptions, described Thursday's event as spectacular but less severe than the earlier ones, noting there was still smoke but nothing as dramatic as before. About 1,500 people were evacuated as authorities maintained a 20-kilometer exclusion zone around the crater, continuing contingency measures as seismic activity in the area continued to decline.

# Summary

Six people—five men and one woman—were arrested at Dover's departure zone this morning on suspicion of Syria-related terrorism offences, according to West Midlands Police. Four of the men in their 20s are from Birmingham, while a 26-year-old man and 23-year-old woman with no fixed abode were also detained and are being questioned at a police station in the West Midlands. The arrests are part of an ongoing investigation, with police conducting searches at multiple addresses in Birmingham and confirming that the suspects pose no immediate risk to public safety.

# MLS Match Summary

Philadelphia earned their first victory of the season with a 2-1 win over New York City FC, with Vincent Nogueira scoring the decisive goal in stoppage time after goalkeeper Josh Saunders collided with a defender on a cross. David Villa equalized for City in the 55th minute, tapping in the rebound after his team-mate's shot was saved following a back-heel move that started the scoring sequence. In other matches, Houston dominated Montreal 3-0, the Red Bulls drew 2-2 with DC United, San Jose beat Vancouver 1-0, and both New England and Real Salt Lake played scoreless draws.

# Summary

A British woman, Charmain Adusah, 41, was found dead face down in a bath at a hotel in Ghana after lying there for four days, with hotel staff discovering her body after she failed to answer the door. Her husband, Eric Isaiah Adusah, 28, a London-based evangelical preacher, told hotel managers that his pregnant wife was ill and did not want to be disturbed before he left the hotel, and is now being held on suspicion of her murder. Hotel staff used a spare key to check on Mrs. Adusah on March 20 after she did not respond to knocks on the door, leading to the discovery of her body and her husband's subsequent arrest in Ghana.

# Summary

Ethan Czahor, a former aide to Jeb Bush who was fired for offensive tweets, has launched an app called "Clear" that removes posts which might cause problems with a user's current or future employer. The app analyzes tweets using IBM's Watson supercomputer and searches for keywords like 'gay' and swear words before deleting potentially offensive content. Czahor developed the app to help his generation—millennials entering the workforce—manage their online presence, as everything they have posted over the last 10 years remains publicly visible and searchable.

# Summary

Justin Rose finished second at the Masters with a 14-under-par score, which has boosted his confidence and momentum for the rest of the season as he tries to follow Rory McIlroy's example by playing well during the big tournaments from April to September. Rose began the final round four shots behind winner Jordan Spieth and came close on several occasions but ultimately couldn't get closer, feeling comfortable in contention despite his recent struggles on the PGA Tour. He believes the experience of competing at the highest level in a major championship will help him play even better in future events, though he acknowledges there were moments where he could have done better and thinks about the key putts he missed during the round.

# Summary

An unidentified college student managed to escape punishment for underage drinking at a Texas country music festival after she beat a police officer in a game of Rock Paper Scissors. A video posted on the social media site Vine by a user known as Old Row shows the girl surrounded by festival goers facing three officers before challenging them to the game, which she won with a rock against scissors. Following her victory, the girl was embraced by a friend wearing a Tri Delta sorority shirt, and the clip quickly went viral, garnering over 500,000 views on social media.

# Missing Teenage Schoolgirl in Melbourne

Fourteen-year-old Sally Gordon-Smith, a schoolgirl from Keilor East near Melbourne, went missing on Friday afternoon around 3pm after leaving her school. According to Victoria Police, she is described as Caucasian, 150cm tall, with blue eyes and brown shoulder-length hair, and was last seen wearing a blue jeans, black jumper, and black shoes. Police are desperately searching for her and have appealed to the public for any information, expressing concern for her safety due to her young age.

# Summary

Crystal Palace manager Alan Pardew has denied reports that he is interested in raiding his former club Newcastle for goalkeeper Tim Krul, expressing disappointment at the speculation. The 53-year-old, who made the shock decision to leave Newcastle for Palace in January, insists he is happy with current goalkeeper Julian Speroni and maintains there has been no contact with Newcastle or Krul regarding a transfer. Pardew is planning a busy summer in the transfer market as he looks to build on Palace's impressive recent form that has pulled the team away from the relegation zone.

# Summary

British photographer John Daniels, 61, has captured adorable images of puppies posing for the camera in his studio, where he avoids the unpredictable British weather and creates heart-warming shots that are the stuff of dreams for greetings card makers. With decades of experience working with animals, both wild and domestic, Daniels uses careful timing to get each animal at the right age so that young creatures like ducklings will happily snuggle up to kittens and puppies, who don't really know what's going on and appear relaxed and content. His approach is never forced—he uses sounds and treats to attract the animals, understanding that they all react in different ways, and he ensures that each animal looks happy in the final picture.

# Summary

Hilary Border, a 54-year-old mother-of-three from Nottinghamshire, was found guilty of fraud for stealing £20,000 from her 83-year-old dementia-stricken mother, whose care she was responsible for as power of attorney. When the care home fees doubled to £600 per week, Border either paid late or stopped paying altogether, leaving an outstanding debt of £16,000 over two years before care home staff reported her to police in November last year. She was sentenced to 16 months in prison suspended for two years, along with a home curfew and unpaid work, with the judge describing her actions as "wicked" given her breach of trust.

# Summary

David Suchet, best known as the star of the TV series Poirot, is fighting to help his grandson Todd face the future with Tuberous Sclerosis Complex, a rare and incurable genetic disease, while criticizing the NHS for disorganization that delays access to vital treatment. Although those with the condition can live a full life, they may develop tumours in organs such as the brain and heart and suffer from epilepsy and learning difficulties. Behind his smiles as a proud grandfather, Suchet has launched the #Fight4Treatment campaign to ensure his grandson and other rare disease sufferers can access available medications through better NHS treatment processes.

# Summary

Yorkshire have signed Indian international Cheteshwar Pujara until the end of May, cancelling Younis Khan's contract after the Pakistani batsman prioritized being part of Pakistan's potential touring party to Bangladesh over playing in the County Championship. Younis Khan was originally set to be Yorkshire's overseas signing for 2015, but his international commitments effectively ruled him out of any playing time in the LV= County Championship. Pujara, who previously played for Derbyshire, will instead link up with the Division One champions as a highly-rated Test cricketer eager to perform in English cricket.

# Summary

US Army Staff Sergeant Julian McDonald welcomed 4-year-old Belgian Malinois Layka into his Columbus, Ohio home this week after fighting to adopt her for two years. Layka had been shot four times at point-blank range by an enemy fighter with an AK-47 during her eighth overseas military tour with Sgt. McDonald in 2012, resulting in the amputation of her front leg, but she completed the mission before receiving emergency surgery. McDonald felt obligated to bring the heroic dog home, saying she saved his life and he owed it to her to save hers.

# Summary

For years, health experts warned that excess salt contributes to tens of thousands of American deaths annually, recommending a limit of 2,300 milligrams per day; however, new research is challenging this conventional wisdom, with salt skeptics arguing that healthy Americans can safely consume up to 6,000 milligrams daily and that the PURE study found people who conform to the recommended limits actually have more heart trouble because low sodium may stimulate renin production, which has harmful effects on blood vessels. Despite disagreement within the American Heart Association itself, President Elliott Antman stated that "the totality of the evidence strongly suggests that Americans should be lowering their sodium intake," while acknowledging that "everyone agrees that current sodium intake is too high." The federal government is reconsidering its 2015 Dietary Guidelines in light of this conflicting scientific evidence about the true dangers of salt consumption.

# Summary

U.S. lawmakers revealed on Tuesday that DEA agents in Colombia were participating in sex parties with prostitutes funded by drug cartels as early as 2001, years earlier than previously disclosed. According to a DEA report summary released by the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform, there were "literally dozens" of such parties rather than isolated incidents. A separate Justice Department investigation found that 10 agents had attended similar parties between 2005 and 2008, with seven admitting to the misconduct and receiving suspensions of two to 10 days.

# Summary

A 36-year-old Louisiana sheriff's deputy, Cpl. Burt Hazeltine, was ambushed and shot three times while directing school buses near a school zone on US 90 in Paradis, west of New Orleans, in what sheriff's officials described as a road rage incident sparked by an argument over traffic. The suspect, 56-year-old Paul Devillier, a former TSA worker, allegedly became angry when Hazeltine refused to stop traffic to allow him to turn, then returned with a gun and opened fire, hitting the deputy in the face, elbow, and eye. Hazeltine, who has been on the force for 15 years and has two sons and two daughters with his wife Mandy, was transported to the hospital in stable but guarded condition.

# Summary

Lord Janner signed a letter requesting to remain a peer just a week before the Crown Prosecution Service ruled him unfit to face child sex charges due to Alzheimer's disease. Abuse campaigners and politicians have criticized the decision, questioning how the suspected paedophile could be deemed too frail to be brought before court yet apparently well enough to maintain his position in the House of Lords. The controversy has been further fueled by concerns about potential conflicts of interest in the decision-making process and accusations that the case represents an establishment cover-up.

# Summary

Turkish authorities blocked access to Twitter and YouTube after the websites refused to remove images of prosecutor Mehmet Selim Kiraz taken during an armed siege in which he was killed by members of a far-left terrorist group. Before imposing the blocks, Turkish authorities had tried to prevent newspapers from printing the images, accusing them of spreading terrorist propaganda, as the prosecutor's office claimed the images deeply upset his family. Twitter and YouTube both stated they were working to restore access after the court-ordered blocks were implemented due to their refusal to comply with the removal request.

# Summary

David and Victoria Beckham have won a planning battle against their next-door neighbour over proposals to install air-conditioning in five rooms at their £31.5 million west London mansion. The neighbour was furious with the plans, claiming the air-con units would affect the historic character of the Victorian house and the surrounding streets. The Royal Borough of Kensington and Chelsea Council ultimately approved the proposals, determining that the air-conditioning would have minimal impact on the listed building and comply with noise regulations.

# California Drought: Governor Brown Orders 25% Water Cutback

California Governor Jerry Brown ordered a 25 percent cutback in water use by cities and towns on Wednesday as the state faces a fourth summer of drought with no relief in sight. Photos taken Saturday reveal the severe toll the ongoing drought is taking on California's once-vibrant landscape, with lush yards, emerald golf courses, and aquamarine swimming pools drying up as state reservoirs dwindle to just one year's worth of water. The mandatory crackdown targets everyone from homeowners to golf courses and includes rebates for replacing lawns with drought-resistant plants and higher water rates to discourage excessive consumption.

# Summary

Universal's "Furious 7," the final film from late actor Paul Walker, is expected to gross $115 million or more at the North American box office this weekend, making history as the top April opening ever and receiving the widest release in Universal's history with roughly 4,003 theaters, surpassing "Despicable Me 2." The film's global debut is projected to exceed $250 million, with the combination of the franchise's popularity and public interest stemming from Walker's death driving massive awareness and ticket sales.

# Summary

Chelsea midfielder Cesc Fabregas has struggled in the second half of the 2015 season, delivering a poor performance in Spain's 2-0 loss to Holland after playing the full 90 minutes, which has concerned manager Jose Mourinho as the midfielder's traditional late-season slump has caused a dramatic decline in his assist statistics. Barcelona's statement upon his departure highlighted Fabregas' unwanted reputation for a downward trend in stats every season, and though he laid on an assist for Diego Costa in Chelsea's recent win over Hull City, his form must improve if he is to prove doubters wrong and help Chelsea secure the Premier League title. With nine games remaining in the season, Mourinho will be hoping Fabregas finds form again and experiences a late-season resurgence to demonstrate that this campaign could be different from his history of mid-season collapses.

# Summary

Victims of a horrific medical experiment conducted by the United States in Guatemala from 1945 to 1956, where officials deliberately infected approximately 700 individuals with syphilis to test drugs and study the disease, are now suing Johns Hopkins University for $1 billion. The lawsuit includes 750 plaintiffs—victims and their families—claiming that Johns Hopkins officials approved, planned, and monitored the study, which targeted vulnerable populations including orphans, soldiers, prisoners, and mental patients, most of whom were children and unaware of what was happening to them. Many victims died from syphilis while others who survived passed the disease to their children, resulting in babies born blind, severely handicapped, or even without brains.

# Summary

Hip-hop star Nelly (Cornell Haynes), 40, was arrested on drug charges in Tennessee after a state trooper stopped his Prevost motor coach on Interstate 40 and noticed an odor of marijuana emitting from the vehicle. According to the Tennessee Department of Safety and Homeland Security, troopers searched the bus and found methamphetamine rocks, marijuana, drug paraphernalia, and several handguns including a gold-plated .50-caliber Desert Eagle pistol, a .45-caliber Taurus pistol, and a .500 Smith & Wesson magnum. Nelly was charged with felony possession of drugs, simple possession of marijuana, and possession of drug paraphernalia, but later posted bond and was released from Putnam County Jail.

# Summary

A Japanese woman took revenge on her cheating boyfriend by dumping his entire Apple collection—including an iMac (starting at £899), iPhone, iPad, and accessories—in a bathtub and sent him photos of the damage. The water damage is likely irreversible, as even the recommended trick of leaving gadgets in rice for 48 hours probably won't dry out this much damage. The incident went viral on Twitter with mixed reactions, though many supported the woman's actions as an effective way to make her unfaithful boyfriend think twice about cheating.

# Summary

Bayern Munich defeated Borussia Dortmund 1-0 on Saturday in a Bundesliga clash at the Westfalenstadion, with Robert Lewandowski scoring the decisive goal. Manager Jurgen Klopp conceded that Bayern deserved the victory, telling Sky that his team lacked punch in the final third and failed to create enough chances. However, Dortmund captain Mats Hummels disagreed with his coach's assessment, arguing that his side created enough opportunities to merit at least a draw against the title-chasing Bavarians.

# Summary

President Obama visited Jamaica on Thursday, becoming the first U.S. president to visit since Ronald Reagan in 1982, where he received an enthusiastic welcome from Prime Minister Portia Simpson Miller and the Jamaican people. While in the region, Obama signaled he will soon remove Cuba from the U.S. list of state sponsors of terrorism, a major step toward normalizing relations ahead of his meeting with Cuban President Raul Castro at the Summit of the Americas in Panama. The move represents a significant shift in U.S. policy after more than 50 years of isolation, though it faces opposition from some members of Congress, particularly those representing Cuban-American constituents in Florida.

# Summary

During his Easter Mass address, Pope Francis reminded the world of the Vatican's status as a state and his role as a moral diplomat while calling for an end to violence and suffering across multiple global conflicts. Foremost in his plea was a request for bloodshed to cease in Iraq and Syria, humanitarian aid to reach those in need, and for the international community to address the refugee crisis. The Pope also called for peace in the Holy Land, Ukraine, Libya, Yemen, Nigeria, and South Sudan, while addressing issues of economic oppression, human trafficking, and drug trafficking.

# Summary

Irina Shayk, 29, recently split from footballer Cristiano Ronaldo after five years together, but she looks anything but down in Linda Farrow eyewear's Spring Summer 15 campaign. The steamy video shows the Russian supermodel being filmed writhing around an LA pool with hunky male model Jarrod Scott while modeling the latest designer shades. The campaign, titled Wanderlust, features Irina in plunging metallic swimsuits and retro-inspired sunglasses in metallic and liquid gold styles.

# Summary

Polish aristocrat Janek Zylinski, whose father was a cavalry officer who led a victorious charge against the Nazis in Kaluszyn in 1939, posted an online video challenging Nigel Farage to a sword fight over his attacks on immigration and discrimination against Polish people. In the video, Zylinski introduced a gold sculpture of his father and offered an alternative "duel of words" if Farage's sword was "a little rusty." The challenge came as Farage defended his immigration stance during a factory visit in Essex, where he met an Eastern European worker and acknowledged the genuine skills gap in British manufacturing.

# Calbuco Volcano Eruption Summary

The Calbuco volcano in southern Chile erupted twice in two days, shooting billions of cubic meters of ash more than six miles into the air and covering towns up to 18 miles away in thick dust, prompting the evacuation of 4,000 people and raising serious health concerns about respiratory damage and contaminated air. According to volcanologists, previous eruptions at Calbuco have lasted up to a week, and the current one could unleash an astonishing ten billion cubic meters of ash into the atmosphere, with ash potentially reaching as far as Australia and Argentina. Residents remain stuck under layers of ash that won't clear, with only one person reported missing since the first explosion yesterday, while President Bachelet declared a state of emergency due to the volcano's unpredictable nature and dangerous proximity to populated areas near Puerto Varas and Puerto Montt.

# Summary

Dani Alves' contract situation at Barcelona is difficult to navigate, with competing narratives emerging from his camp and the club over whether a new deal includes one, two, or three years with various sub-clauses and incentives. Manchester United are prime suspects to offer him a straightforward three-year deal, which would be a perfect way to round off his remarkable Barcelona career of 13 years, four leagues, and two Champions League titles. Barcelona's incompetence in finding a replacement and their FIFA transfer ban mean they are desperate to keep Alves, though if they allow him to leave they would have to rely on Douglas, who started just one game and looks completely out of his depth.

# Summary

Kate Temple, a 33-year-old hairdresser, was jailed for 20 months after she bit off a portion of Shelley Williams' ear during an altercation at a charity hog roast event at Dawdon Cricket Club in Seaham, County Durham. The incident occurred after Williams accused Temple of having an affair with her partner and threw a drink over her, which escalated into a scuffle that left Williams covered in blood. The victim underwent reconstructive surgery and has suffered lasting psychological and physical damage, becoming self-conscious and struggling to leave her house, as confirmed when police found the missing ear the next day.

# Summary

Jenson Button believes McLaren has made significant progress with their new Honda power unit and is now competitive alongside midfield rivals, though both he and Fernando Alonso retired from the Malaysian Grand Prix due to failures rather than lack of pace. Button said the team "still has a long way to go" and needs more downforce, but is pleased they are "moving in the right direction" and can now race other cars instead of "trotting around at the back." Despite acknowledging cooler weather in Shanghai could present challenges, Button is optimistic about the team's progress and hopes they will "start picking off" competitors in the races ahead.

# Summary

Claudia Martins, a 33-year-old Portuguese national, has been convicted of manslaughter by reason of diminished responsibility after suffocating her newborn baby daughter by filling the infant's mouth with toilet paper. Martins secretly gave birth alone at her sister's flat in Bristol and hid the baby's body in a suitcase for three days before paramedics were called after finding her sitting in a bath covered in blood. She was acquitted of murder but the jury found her guilty of manslaughter, and she now awaits sentencing.

# Summary

AP McCoy will ride Mr Mole in the Grade One Celebration Chase at Sandown on Saturday, which has been installed as one of three 7-2 co-favourites alongside Special Tiara and Nicky Henderson's Sprinter Sacre (ridden by Nico de Boinville in the absence of injured Barry Geraghty). Mr Mole will be McCoy's last ride in a Grade One race, facing six rivals in the seven-runner line-up. Ironically, McCoy announced his retirement after riding Mr Mole to victory at Newbury in February.

# Summary

A woman in her 20s, who cannot swim, was rescued from the River Cam in Cambridge after she lost her footing and fell from a punt when it collided with another boat. Two of her friends immediately leapt into the water to save her, and she was pulled to safety by rescuers, after which she could be seen holding her head in her hands in shock. The incident occurred on a day when Britain experienced unseasonably warm Easter weather, with temperatures reaching nearly 21°C, prompting families across the country to flock to beaches and parks.

# Summary

The article argues that the performances of Jason Holder in Antigua and Marlon Samuels in the second Test will inspire their teammates and help new West Indies coach Phil Simmons create a culture of discipline, character, and application—much like how Mike Atherton inspired others through his determined batting to save a Test match. The author defends Test cricket against criticism that matches like Antigua are boring, explaining that the five-day format offers greater mental and physical challenges and provides far more satisfaction than shorter formats like the IPL. He emphasizes that West Indies must first learn to stay in matches and instill pride and discipline before they can become a winning side and retain their best players.

# Summary

Officer Michael Slager claimed he shot Walter Scott, a 50-year-old man, in self-defense during a struggle over a Taser, stating his actions were in line with procedure; however, witness video footage contradicts this account by showing Slager shooting Scott in the back from 15 to 20 feet away as Scott attempted to run. The video also reveals that Slager retrieved the fallen Taser and placed it next to Scott's body, and shows officers never actually administering CPR despite their claims in incident reports. The discrepancies between police accounts and the bystander video suggest a potential cover-up, prompting concerns about whether the shooting would have remained hidden without the video evidence.

# Summary

Nina Moric, a 38-year-old Croatian model, denied Italian media reports that she had attempted suicide, instead telling her many fans that she collapsed at home due to low blood pressure and was treated in hospital after falling and hitting her head. Her boyfriend initially confirmed the suicide claims, but Moric clarified that she only needed a bit of rest to recover from the fall, stating she was found by her mother in their Milan apartment. Moric, who was once married to famous paparazzi photographer Fabrizio Corona and shares a son with him, reassured followers that she is well and has no intention of taking her own life.

# Summary

Juventus secured their route to the Champions League semi-finals with a 0-0 draw against Monaco at Stade Louis II on Wednesday night, progressing 1-0 on aggregate following Arturo Vidal's penalty in Turin the previous week. Patrice Evra, who withstood nervous moments as Juventus sat back and allowed the French side to dictate play, described the Italian side's performance as "ugly" but effective, crediting their quintessential Italian defensive style for the victory. The former Manchester United full-back also urged his compatriot Paul Pogba to avoid rushing his return from a hamstring injury and only come back when fully fit.

# Summary

At Omaha's Henry Doorley Zoo, a little girl started beating her chest at a gorilla through a pane of glass, but the Silverback who had been standing at the back of the enclosure decided enough was enough and ran at the onlookers. The massive animal launched himself with hundreds of pounds of force at the glass, which cracked on impact, causing the terrified family to flee to safety. Since the incident was uploaded to YouTube, the video has received over 126,000 views.

# Summary

John Suchet, a 71-year-old Classic FM presenter, is mourning the death of his wife Bonnie, who passed away peacefully on April 15, 2015, at age 73, following a decade-long battle with dementia. A death notice described her as the "adored wife of John" and "devoted mother of Alec and Hereward," reflecting the couple's relationship which Suchet had previously described as being "made in heaven." Suchet has been openly vocal about the devastating impact of Bonnie's illness on their relationship, writing a book about his experience and becoming honorary president of Dementia UK charity.

# Summary

Ben and Shelby Offrink met in a college study group for calculus-based physics in Michigan, where Ben cleverly arranged to have it be just the two of them by telling other people not to come. Eight years later, after marrying and having two young daughters, both Ben and Shelby were diagnosed with aggressive cancers—Shelby with a rare spinal glioblastoma in 2014 and Ben with a recurrence of Hodgkin's lymphoma that same year. Despite facing tremendous challenges with chemotherapy, paralysis, and Shelby's cancer spreading to her brain, the couple remains determined to fight their diseases and seek advanced treatment options at major medical centers for the sake of their family.

# Summary

Real Madrid's attacking prowess is safe and present after their nine-goal demolition of Granada, but Carlo Ancelotti now faces the enviable dilemma of fitting his array of stars into his starting line-up. However, with seven attacking players competing for six spots in his preferred 4-3-3 formation, one player is set to be upset at being left out of the club's best XI. For the first time since November, the midfield quartet of Toni Kroos, Luka Modric, and James Rodriguez are all fit alongside the famed 'BBC' triumvirate of Bale, Benzema, and Ronaldo, and Marca also recognises that Ancelotti will likely choose James Rodriguez over Isco due to the former's superior goal and assist statistics this season.

# Summary

Despite stepping up his preparation for the highly-anticipated blockbuster fight against Manny Pacquiao on May 2 in Las Vegas, Floyd Mayweather still found time to pose for a picture with Motown legend Gladys Knight during a training session. Tickets for the Fight of the Century at the MGM Grand sold out within 60 seconds on Ticketmaster last week, making it the biggest event in boxing history. Mayweather shared the moment on Twitter, thanking Knight for coming to support him at the gym.

# Summary

Ed Miliband, who has long struggled to convince voters of his suitability for prime minister, has hired a US-based leadership coaching firm called ExtendedMind to help him overcome anxiety and develop his communication skills using neuroscience and business psychology. The consultants work with politicians to improve their "authenticity" and leadership abilities through coaching on body language, breathing, and messaging, though critics question the expense given his already-substantial investment in Obama adviser David Axelrod. The hiring comes after Miliband's widely-publicized gaffes, including forgetting to mention the deficit in a conference speech and relying heavily on a lectern during campaign appearances.

# Summary

Raymond Townsend, a married manager at US Limousine on Long Island, New York, was found liable for sexual harassment after firing employee Geralyn Ganci for refusing his repeated sexual advances over more than a year. Townsend explicitly stated in a text message that Ganci was fired because she "refused to have sex with the General Manager," along with sending numerous other sexually explicit messages and physically assaulting her. A federal judge ordered Townsend and US Limousine to pay Ganci over $700,000 in damages and legal fees.

# Summary

Bayern Munich midfielder Thiago made his first appearance in over a year after suffering a serious knee injury in March 2014, coming on as a substitute in Bayern's 1-0 victory against Borussia Dortmund at the Westfalenstadion on Saturday. The Spanish playmaker expressed his gratitude to the German club, coaches, and teammates for their support during his recovery, describing the emotional moment as a reminder that "football is my life." Although acknowledging he is still not match-fit and felt tired after just 10 minutes, Thiago remains focused on winning games and titles as Bayern pursue the treble.

# Summary

David Cameron accused the BBC of peddling "bollocks" after presenter Andrew Marr incorrectly claimed on Sunday that the Tory leader's favourite sport was foxhunting, leaving Cameron baffled by the question during the interview. In response, Cameron took to a wooden pallet to address party supporters, echoing John Major's election tactics, though he was heckled about the NHS during his speech. Marr later admitted on Twitter that he had made an error, apologizing for the mistake while Cameron clarified that his favourite sport is actually tennis.

# Summary

Gwyneth Paltrow, an actress turned lifestyle guru known for promoting detoxes on her site Goop.com, is bringing awareness to the difficulties of life on food stamps by participating in the #FoodBankNYCChallenge, which requires spending just $29 for a week's worth of groceries. Celebrity chef Mario Batali nominated Paltrow for the challenge, urging participants to "walk in someone else's shoes" to better understand the struggles families face on food assistance. The challenge was created in response to recent Congressional cuts to food stamps, which have led to an immediate increase in visitors to soup kitchens and food pantries.

# Summary

Dozens of staff at Manchester United's Megastore, including mascot Fred the Red, are anxious about their job security as Nike's £750m kit deal with the club expires on July 31. Some workers claim they are being kept in the dark about whether they will be re-employed after Nike's agreement with the club ends, with many blaming the US sportswear company for the uncertainty. However, Nike has stated they are in constant communication with Manchester United and are engaged in a consultation process with employees to manage the transition.

# Summary

Liverpool edged past Blackburn Rovers in their FA Cup sixth round replay to reach the semi-finals, with Philippe Coutinho breaking the deadlock after 161 goalless minutes across the two games. Despite the goal, Blackburn came close to levelling matters when goalkeeper Simon Eastwood was denied a chance to equalize in stoppage time after performing well throughout the match and even attempting a shot himself. Coutinho was named Man of the Match for his mercurial midfield display, which proved decisive in securing Liverpool's passage to Wembley.

# Summary

A heart-warming video created by Pandora to celebrate Mother's Day features six blindfolded children who are asked to pick their mother out of a group of six women using only touch and scent. The sweet youngsters tentatively move along the line, touching the women's hands, hair, and faces until each successfully recognizes and embraces their own mother. The touching video has gone viral with 2.8 million views, even attracting celebrity attention, as it beautifully demonstrates the unique bond between mother and child.

# Summary

Ian Watson, a 38-year-old Salford assistant coach, is preparing to come out of playing retirement to answer an emergency call from the Red Devils after the team lost a fourth player to suspension and suffered multiple injuries. Watson has been stepping up his training ahead of Sunday's home game against Castleford and is ready to play if needed, which would make him the oldest player currently in Super League. The injuries include scrum-half Michael Dobson (knee), winger Ben Jones-Bishop (leg), and Cory Paterson among others, leaving Salford severely depleted for the match.

# Summary

At the 79th Masters, 21-year-old Jordan Spieth marked one up for the future of golf over its past and present by capturing first-day honours with a stunning opening round of 64, establishing himself as the youngest first-round leader in Masters history with a three-shot lead. For much of the day, it appeared that older competitors like 34-year-old Justin Rose and 45-year-old Ernie Els might challenge for the top spot with impressive scores of 67, but Spieth's brilliant play, including six birdies in seven holes and a 25-foot birdie putt at the 18th, separated him decisively from the field on a spectacular day of scoring. While other favored players like defending champion Bubba Watson and Rory McIlroy opened with respectable rounds of 71 and 70, they found themselves significantly behind Spieth's exceptional performance.

# Summary

England captain Alastair Cook faces a crucial three-Test series against West Indies beginning Monday, with defeat being unthinkable for his career prospects. Cook must shut out the distracting noise surrounding English cricket, including the demise of Paul Downton, speculation over the new director of cricket, and ongoing Kevin Pietersen drama, to concentrate on what should be a winnable series. Despite the seismic changes happening around the team, Cook and coach Peter Moores cannot afford any slip-ups in this assignment, which is vital for the captain's future.

# Summary

The Pentagon announced Tuesday plans to exhume and try to identify the remains of nearly 400 sailors and Marines who were killed when the USS Oklahoma was hit by nine torpedoes during the bombing of Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941. After the attack, only 35 of the 429 sailors and Marines onboard were identified, with hundreds buried as unknowns and later reburied in 1950 at the National Memorial Cemetery of the Pacific—also known as Punchbowl—inside a volcanic crater in Honolulu, Hawaii. The identification effort is now possible due to advances in forensic science and genealogical support from family members, offering long-awaited closure to families who previously could not get information about where their loved ones were buried.

# Summary

YouTuber Yousef Saleh Erakat disguised himself as a homeless man and approached people on the street offering them money to explore society's attitudes toward wealth and class differences. The video revealed that most people reacted negatively when offered money by someone appearing poor, with many becoming angry or insulted, while only a few displayed genuine kindness and humility in response. Erakat's social experiment aimed to question whether charity is socially acceptable only when the wealthy help the poor, rather than examining people's true character and compassion regardless of perceived status.

# Summary

Katherine Hadsell, grandmother of missing 18-year-old Longwood University student Anjelica Hadsell, criticized Norfolk authorities for doing nothing to find her granddaughter, who has been missing since March 2. Katherine expressed frustration with police and demanded they provide more insight into their actual search efforts. The case has become complicated by the arrest of Anjelica's stepfather Wesley Hadsell on charges related to breaking into a home he suspected held his stepdaughter, though police have not named him as an official suspect and continue to search a pond for evidence of her disappearance.

# Summary

Researchers at the Royal Melbourne Institute of Technology have developed Joggobot, a drone designed to fly alongside joggers as a companion to help maintain pace and motivation during runs. The team tested the quadcopter with 13 joggers and found that the drone effectively acted as a "pace keeper," creating a sense of peer pressure similar to running with a human companion, and could potentially be adapted for other activities like cycling, cross-country skiing, and rowing. While some runners expressed safety concerns and found the experience awkward with a camera attached, most appreciated the drone's ability to keep them running at a consistent pace, offering an alternative to traditional jogging companions like dogs or fitness trackers.

# Summary

Matthew Bird from New Zealand quit his well-paid job as an agricultural adviser and halved his income from $70,000 to $38,000 annually to avoid a $600-a-month hike in child support payments. After Inland Revenue amended their child support formula in April, Bird's monthly payments increased from $1,300 to $1,900, prompting him to switch careers so his payments would drop to $554 per month instead. The extreme measure highlights growing concerns among parents about the new child support calculations, with some MPs offering assistance to those struggling with the increased payments.

# Summary

Navinder Singh Sarao, a British trader accused of causing the 2010 Wall Street "Flash Crash" that wiped out nearly a trillion dollars, was arrested by Scotland Yard's extradition unit at the FBI's request after allegedly using fraudulent trading software to manipulate markets for six years. The CFTC brought civil charges alongside criminal charges by the Department of Justice, though experts remain puzzled why it took so many years to act despite the CME warning Sarao about illicit trades as early as 2009; one lawyer suggests a whistleblower's involvement was likely essential in finally uncovering the manipulation. Operating from his modest Hounslow home, Sarao allegedly established an offshore Caribbean company called "Nav Sarao Milking Markets" and accumulated £26 million through illegal trades, bewildering neighbors who knew nothing of his supposed wealth.

# Summary

Lisa Webb, a Canadian expat and former assistant elementary school principal who moved to France in 2009, is the author of the blog Canadian Expat Mom. When her vegetable-phobic daughter started school in France, Lisa was surprised to discover sophisticated three-course meals on the menu that she had only eaten in fancy restaurants with her husband. After being offered the chance to observe her daughter's lunch break, Lisa witnessed how French schoolchildren politely ate everything served to them using proper cutlery, even though her own daughter had refused vegetables at home.

# Summary

Shopping with friends encourages overspending, with 64 percent of adults admitting they spend more money when shopping in groups due to peer pressure and the urge to show off. Despite 85 percent of shoppers setting budgets, 95 percent end up spending more than planned, particularly on food, clothing, and household items, often justifying their purchases by claiming items were on sale or treating themselves as a special reward.

# Summary

Ellie Grant, 22, appeared on ITV's This Morning to discuss how she suddenly developed Tourette's syndrome at age 21 and found love with her deaf fiancé Jordan, who manages her outbursts by switching off his hearing aid. During the pre-recorded interview, Grant experienced a verbal tic and blurted out "silver fox" at presenter Phillip Schofield, his nickname, which Amanda Holden kindly acknowledged as "a good one to have." The couple met in a pub where Grant's initial symptoms included involuntary outbursts like shouting "marshmallow" and "Japan," and they have since moved in together with Jordan serving as her carer.

# Summary

The White House announced Thursday that Adam Gadahn, 36, an American citizen known as the "American mouthpiece of al Qaeda," was killed in January in a U.S. counterterrorism operation, though he was not specifically targeted. Gadahn, who converted to Islam in 1995 and became one of al Qaeda's most prominent members, rose through the ranks as the terrorist organization's chief English-language propagandist, appearing in numerous videos calling for attacks against American and Western interests and encouraging lone-wolf terrorism. According to documents recovered from Osama bin Laden's compound, Gadahn had taken on an increasingly prominent institutional role within al Qaeda, including making recommendations for the organization's media strategy.

# Summary

Formula One driver Jenson Button, 35, completed the London Marathon in 2 hours, 52 minutes and 30 seconds, telling the BBC he was "chuffed to bits" with his performance, though he joked he was disappointed to finish behind former Olympic rower James Cracknell. Running for Cancer Research, Button praised the amazing atmosphere and collective spirit of the 36,000 runners, describing the experience as "really really emotional." He emphasized how people's efforts and the goodwill at the event made the whole experience incredibly moving.
